label: component of
description: Basis risk is one of the four IRRBB sub-risks — it captures the exposure from imperfect correlation between the reference rates on assets and liabilities, which is invisible to repricing gap and duration gap analysis.

label: impacts
description: Basis risk impacts NII when the reference rates on assets and liabilities diverge — even if both sides reprice at the same time, a widening spread between reference rates squeezes net interest income.

label: amplified by
description: The LIBOR-to-SOFR transition amplified basis risk as banks migrated assets and liabilities to different replacement rates at different speeds, creating new and sometimes unexpected basis exposures.

label: component of
description: The curvature risk charge is one of the three components of the FRTB Sensitivity-Based Method — it captures second-order price sensitivity for options and structured products that is not covered by the linear delta charge.

label: quantifies regulatory cost of
description: The curvature risk charge is the regulatory capital measure for convexity risk — just as bond convexity measures the second-order price sensitivity to yield, the curvature charge measures the second-order P&L deviation from the linear delta approximation for options.

label: income metric of
description: EaR is the income-based IRRBB metric that complements EVE — it shows the near-term earnings impact of rate changes, which is what bank boards and equity analysts focus on.

label: measures risk to
description: EaR quantifies the potential decline in NII under adverse rate scenarios — it is the probabilistic wrapper around NII sensitivity that translates rate risk into an earnings impact figure.

label: analogous to
description: EaR is the banking book analogue of VaR — both express worst-case losses at a confidence level, but VaR applies to mark-to-market P&L while EaR applies to accrual-based net interest income.

label: solvency metric of
description: EVE is the present-value (solvency) lens of IRRBB — it captures how rate changes affect the economic net worth of the banking book, complementing the near-term NII view.

label: sensitive to
description: EVE is affected by repricing mismatches because assets and liabilities with different repricing dates respond differently to rate changes, creating present-value gains or losses.

label: driven by
description: The duration gap is the primary determinant of EVE sensitivity — the larger the mismatch between asset and liability durations, the more EVE changes for a given rate shock.

label: includes
description: The FRTB Sensitivity-Based Method has three components — delta, vega, and curvature charges — summed to give total SBM capital; the curvature charge captures the second-order (convexity) risk not covered by the delta sensitivity.

label: maps sensitivities to
description: The FRTB SBM requires all interest rate delta sensitivities to be mapped to the ten prescribed tenor vertices before aggregation — these vertices are the buckets used within the GIRR risk class of the sensitivity-based method.

label: complements
description: The FRTB SBM is the standardised capital charge that all banks must compute, whilst Expected Shortfall is the metric used by the Internal Models Approach — the two sit alongside each other in the FRTB framework as standardised and model-based capital floors.

label: measured via
description: EVE is one of the two core IRRBB metrics — it captures the present-value impact of rate changes on the entire banking book, providing the solvency perspective that Basel requires alongside the earnings (NII) view.

label: decomposed into
description: Repricing risk is the most fundamental component of IRRBB — the maturity and repricing mismatches between banking book assets and liabilities are the primary source of interest rate exposure.

label: hedged using
description: Interest rate swaps are the primary tool for managing IRRBB — paying or receiving fixed in a swap alters the duration profile of the banking book, allowing ALM teams to adjust the duration gap without changing the underlying loan and deposit portfolio.

label: defines buckets for
description: The ten prescribed tenor vertices (0.25Y to 30Y) are the interest rate buckets used within the FRTB SBM's GIRR risk class — sensitivities are mapped to these vertices and then aggregated using prescribed correlations to produce the IR delta capital charge.

label: receives sensitivity from
description: Under FRTB, each position's DV01 (dollar value of a basis point) is assigned to the nearest prescribed tenor vertex — the vertex then accumulates the net DV01 from all positions, which becomes the sensitivity input s_k in the capital aggregation formula.

label: adds duration to
description: Government bonds held in the HQLA buffer add duration to the asset side of the balance sheet, increasing EVE sensitivity to rate rises — the 2022 UK gilt crisis showed how LCR buffers can themselves become a source of mark-to-market loss.

label: numerator of
description: The HQLA stock — classified into Level 1, 2A, and 2B with haircuts and caps — forms the numerator of the LCR calculation, determining how much of the bank's liquid assets count toward the regulatory minimum.

label: mitigates
description: The liquidity buffer is the bank's primary defence against short-term funding stress — its composition and size determine whether the bank can meet obligations without fire-selling illiquid assets.

label: constrains
description: The LCR requirement forces banks to hold large HQLA buffers that add duration to the asset side, creating an interest rate position that ALM must manage alongside the rest of the banking book.

label: floors run-off of
description: Basel's LCR assigns prescribed run-off rates to different deposit categories, establishing a regulatory floor for how stable deposits can be assumed to be — this interacts with the behavioural assumptions used in NMD modelling.

label: mitigates
description: The LCR is the primary regulatory tool for mitigating short-term liquidity risk — by requiring banks to hold sufficient HQLA to cover 30 days of stressed outflows, it ensures a minimum buffer against funding crises.

label: complements
description: The NSFR addresses structural funding stability over a 1-year horizon, complementing the LCR's 30-day stress focus — together they form Basel III's twin liquidity standards.

label: constrains funding mix of
description: The NSFR forces ALM teams to maintain a minimum proportion of stable funding, constraining the maturity transformation strategy and often requiring more expensive long-term debt issuance or retail deposit gathering.

label: mitigates structural
description: The NSFR mitigates structural liquidity risk by requiring stable funding for illiquid assets, preventing the maturity mismatch that caused failures like Northern Rock where long-term mortgages were funded by overnight wholesale markets.

label: results from failing
description: A risk factor is classified as non-modellable precisely when it fails the Risk Factor Eligibility Test — the NMRF designation is the direct consequence of having fewer than 24 real price observations or a gap exceeding 90 days in the prior 12 months.

label: charged via stressed
description: NMRFs receive a separate stressed Expected Shortfall capital charge computed from a single worst-case scenario rather than from historical simulation — this stressed ES is typically far larger than the equivalent IMA charge, reflecting the lack of observable market data.

label: distorts
description: Optionality risk distorts EVE calculations because embedded options create non-linear rate sensitivity — mortgage prepayment shortens asset duration when rates fall, reducing the EVE gain the bank would otherwise receive.

label: component of
description: Optionality risk is one of the four IRRBB sub-risks — it captures the asymmetric exposure from embedded options in banking products that customers exercise adversely for the bank.

label: driven by
description: Non-maturity deposits are a key source of optionality risk — depositors' behavioural option to withdraw creates an embedded option that shortens liability duration when rates rise.

label: impacts
description: Repricing mismatches also affect EVE because assets and liabilities with different repricing dates have different present-value sensitivities to rate changes.

label: component of
description: Repricing risk is the most fundamental of the four IRRBB sub-risks (alongside yield curve, basis, and optionality risk) — it captures the core maturity mismatch exposure in the banking book.

label: impacts
description: Repricing risk directly impacts NII — when rate-sensitive assets and liabilities reprice at different times, rate movements create gaps that cause NII to rise or fall.

label: feeds into
description: Modellable risk factors that pass the RFET are included in the Internal Models Approach ES calculation, whilst those that fail are capitalised separately as NMRFs — the RFET therefore determines which risk factors enter the IMA versus which generate the NMRF add-on alongside the SBM floor.

label: classifies
description: The RFET is the gateway test that determines whether a risk factor is modellable or non-modellable — failing the minimum 24-observation threshold triggers NMRF classification and a separate, more punitive capital treatment.

label: component of
description: Yield curve risk is one of the four IRRBB sub-risks — it captures the exposure to non-parallel rate movements that a simple duration gap (parallel shift) analysis would miss.

label: measured at
description: Yield curve risk is measured by computing PV01 sensitivities at each tenor vertex and applying non-parallel shocks — the tenor vertices are the grid points at which curve reshaping is assessed.

label: risk from reshaping of
description: Yield curve risk is the exposure to non-parallel changes in the yield curve — steepening, flattening, and twisting — that affect different tenors of the banking book differently.

label: used with
description: The formulas for extracting the real and imaginary parts of a complex number — Re(z) = (z + z̄)/2 and Im(z) = (z − z̄)/2i — are direct applications of complex addition and subtraction, and are used when computing Fourier inversion integrals in option pricing.

label: extends
description: Addition and subtraction of complex numbers build directly on the definition of a complex number as a + bi, applying the standard field operations separately to the real and imaginary components — a prerequisite for all further complex arithmetic used in pricing models.

label: used in
description: Complex addition is the component-wise operation underlying the superposition principle of the Fourier transform — log-characteristic functions of independent risk factors are added in the complex plane to build the joint characteristic function used in multi-factor option pricing.

label: inverse of
description: An anti-derivative reverses differentiation — where differentiation extracts a rate of change (such as a Greek), integration recovers the original function, underpinning the link between instantaneous sensitivities and accumulated values in derivatives pricing.

label: formalised by
description: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus gives anti-derivatives their power: it states that a definite integral can be evaluated simply by finding any anti-derivative and evaluating it at the bounds, connecting the concept directly to area and accumulated change.

label: uniqueness determined by
description: An anti-derivative contains an arbitrary constant C that is only pinned down by an initial or boundary condition — in finance this corresponds to imposing a terminal payoff or initial value on an ODE or PDE solution.

label: computed via
description: Integration rules — the power rule, substitution, and integration by parts — are the practical toolkit for finding anti-derivatives; without them, computing option prices, present values, and probability integrals would be intractable.

label: computed via
description: Areas under parametric curves are evaluated as definite integrals after substituting dx = f'(t) dt — the same definite integral machinery applies, just with the parameter t as the integration variable.

label: extends
description: Computing areas under parametric curves is the integration extension of parametric equations — once a curve is defined parametrically, the area formula requires converting the x-integral into a t-integral using dx/dt.

label: computed via
description: The area between two curves is a definite integral of their difference over the interval where they are separated — finding intersection points first determines the correct limits of integration.

label: requires solving
description: Finding the intersection points of two curves — where the integrand changes sign — requires solving the two curve equations simultaneously, giving the limits for the area integral.

label: contrasted with
description: Arithmetic and geometric series are the two canonical series types — arithmetic sums use a common difference while geometric sums use a common ratio, and the two formulas are derived and applied side by side in finance when comparing linear and exponential growth.

label: motivates
description: The arithmetic series is a finite sum, and extending the concept to infinitely many terms motivates the study of infinite series convergence — arithmetic series always diverge when extended to infinity, making the contrast with convergent geometric series instructive.

label: described by
description: Big-O notation is the formal language for describing asymptotic behaviour — f(x) = O(g(x)) as x → ∞ means f grows no faster than g asymptotically, quantifying the rate of approach to the asymptote.

label: defined via
description: An asymptote is the limit of a curve as the variable tends to infinity or a particular value — asymptotic behaviour is precisely the language of limits applied to function behaviour at extremes.

label: exhibited by
description: The tangent function has vertical asymptotes at x = π/2 + nπ where cos x = 0 — the most elementary example of an asymptote encountered in A Level trigonometry.

label: error characterised by
description: The truncation error of the backward difference approximation is O(h), meaning it halves when the step size is halved — big-O notation is the standard way to compare the accuracy of forward, backward, and central difference schemes in numerical methods.

label: derived from
description: The backward difference approximation is derived by truncating the Taylor series of f(x - h) after the first derivative term — the Taylor expansion reveals the O(h) truncation error and why the leading error term is proportional to the second derivative.

label: used in
description: The backward difference approximation for the time derivative produces the implicit finite difference scheme for the Black-Scholes PDE — this scheme is unconditionally stable, making it the preferred choice for option pricing grids over the explicit forward scheme.

label: alternative approach in
description: When bumping to compute a Greek numerically, the backward difference (using V(S) and V(S - h)) gives first-order accuracy and requires only one extra pricing per sensitivity — a cost-effective alternative to central difference when backward perturbation is more natural.

label: quantifies error of
description: Big-O notation is the standard way to express the truncation error of a Taylor expansion — writing the remainder as O(h^3) after a quadratic approximation tells practitioners exactly how fast the error shrinks as the step size decreases.

label: characterises error of
description: The Euler-Maruyama scheme has O(sqrt(Δt)) strong convergence and O(Δt) weak convergence — big-O notation is the formal language used to state these rates and to compare numerical SDE discretisation schemes.

label: coefficients of
description: Binomial coefficients C(n,k) are precisely the coefficients of each term in the binomial expansion (a+b)^n — the name 'binomial coefficient' reflects this direct relationship.

label: defined using
description: The binomial coefficient C(n,k) = n!/(k!(n-k)!) is defined entirely in terms of factorials — computing it requires evaluating three factorials and dividing.

label: arranged in
description: Pascal's triangle is a visual arrangement of all binomial coefficients — row n lists C(n,0) through C(n,n), and each entry is the sum of the two above it by Pascal's identity.

label: counts paths in
description: In the CRR binomial tree, C(n,k) counts the number of paths leading to the node with exactly k up-moves after n steps — it is the combinatorial weight that converts single-path probabilities into the terminal distribution.

label: weights
description: The binomial distribution probability P(X=k) = C(n,k) p^k (1-p)^(n-k) uses the binomial coefficient as the combinatorial weight counting the number of sequences with exactly k successes in n trials.

label: uses
description: Every binomial coefficient in the expansion is computed using factorials — the formula C(n,k) = n!/(k!(n-k)!) is the combinatorial core of the binomial theorem, making factorial the algebraic building block of the entire expansion.

label: special case of
description: The binomial expansion is a special case of the Taylor series applied to f(x) = (1+x)^alpha — the generalised binomial series is precisely the Taylor expansion of this function, and first-order approximations such as sqrt(1+x) ≈ 1 + x/2 are truncated Taylor series.

label: approximates factors in
description: The CRR binomial tree up and down factors u = e^(σ√δt) and d = e^(−σ√δt) are derived by applying the first-order binomial approximation to the exponential — the binomial expansion is the bridge between the exact CRR formula and the simpler additive form.

label: generates probabilities of
description: Expanding (p + (1-p))^n via the binomial theorem directly yields the probability mass function of the binomial distribution — each term C(n,k) p^k (1-p)^(n-k) is one term of the expansion, summing to 1 over all k.

label: averages with
description: The centred derivative is the average of the forward and backward difference approximations, which is why its leading error terms cancel and it achieves second-order accuracy.

label: more accurate than
description: The centred derivative (f(x+h) − f(x−h))/(2h) has O(h²) error versus O(h) for the forward difference — doubling accuracy at the same step size, making it preferred for Greek calculations in finite-difference option pricing.

label: derived from
description: The centred derivative formula is derived by writing the Taylor expansions of f(x+h) and f(x−h), subtracting, and dividing by 2h — the odd terms survive and the even error terms cancel, explaining the improved O(h²) accuracy.

label: computes
description: Every Greek is a partial derivative of the option price — computing how Delta changes with the underlying (Gamma) or how a multi-leg portfolio s risk decomposes requires repeated application of the chain rule.

label: extended by
description: Ito s Lemma is the stochastic generalisation of the chain rule — it adds the correction term (1/2)sigma^2 S^2 f_{SS} dt that arises because (dW)^2 = dt, making it the essential tool for differentiating functions of stochastic processes.

label: derives
description: The quadratic formula is derived by applying completing the square to the general quadratic ax² + bx + c = 0, so the technique is the foundation of the formula.

label: used in derivation of
description: Deriving the Black-Scholes closed-form solution requires completing the square in the exponent of the lognormal density to separate the integral into N(d₁) and N(d₂) terms.

label: enables integration of
description: Evaluating Gaussian integrals requires completing the square in the exponent to reduce the integrand to a standard normal density — this is the central algebraic step in deriving probabilities from the normal distribution.

label: gives rise to
description: The definition of the complex conjugate directly implies the modulus identity, real-part extraction formula, and conjugate symmetry — these named identities are the algebraic tools applied in Fourier inversion and characteristic-function option pricing.

label: operation on
description: The complex conjugate is a fundamental operation on complex numbers — negating the imaginary part produces the conjugate pair whose product is the squared modulus, a property exploited whenever real-valued quantities must be extracted from complex-valued integrands in Fourier pricing.

label: negates part of
description: Conjugation works by negating the coefficient of the imaginary unit i — understanding what i represents is a prerequisite for grasping why conjugation reflects a complex number across the real axis in the complex plane.

label: extends
description: Complex conjugate identities are the algebraic consequences of the conjugate definition — they formalise the modulus, real-part, and multiplicative properties as named results that can be applied directly in Fourier inversion and characteristic-function computations.

label: enables efficiency of
description: The modulus identity $z\bar{z} = |z|^2$ and conjugate symmetry allow FFT-based option pricers to discard half the computed frequencies, directly halving the computational cost of the Carr-Madan pricing algorithm.

label: simplifies
description: The conjugate symmetry identity $\varphi(-u) = \overline{\varphi(u)}$ halves the integration domain in the Fourier inversion formula for option prices, which is why FFT-based pricers such as Carr-Madan integrate only over non-negative frequencies.

label: used in
description: The conjugate symmetry property of characteristic functions — a direct consequence of the multiplicative conjugate identity — is the key structural feature exploited when numerically inverting characteristic functions to recover option prices or probability densities.

label: extends
description: The characteristic function E[e^{iuX}] is the complex-variable generalisation of the moment generating function — replacing the real argument with iu ensures the expectation always exists for fat-tailed distributions where the MGF diverges.

label: used in
description: The Heston model's semi-analytic pricing formula is derived by inverting the characteristic function of log(S_T) via numerical integration in the complex plane — complex exponentials are the core computational tool for this Fourier-based approach.

label: differentiated by
description: The chain rule is the differentiation rule for composite functions: (f∘g)'(x) = f'(g(x))·g'(x), making composite functions the fundamental motivation for the chain rule.

label: domain determined by
description: The domain of a composite function f∘g requires that x is in the domain of g AND g(x) is in the domain of f, making domain analysis essential for valid composition.

label: undone by
description: The inverse function f⁻¹ satisfies f⁻¹∘f = identity, so composing a function with its inverse undoes the transformation — the defining property of the inverse.

label: part of
description: The constant of integration C is the defining feature of an indefinite integral — it represents the infinite family of antiderivatives that all differ only by an additive constant, and understanding C is inseparable from understanding what an indefinite integral means.

label: resolved by
description: The constant of integration is uniquely determined by imposing an initial or boundary condition — in Black-Scholes pricing, the terminal payoff condition fixes the free constant and selects the unique solution from the infinite family of antiderivatives.

label: appears in
description: Every indefinite integral produced by the standard integration rules carries an additive constant C — the constant of integration is the unavoidable companion to any antiderivative, reminding the user that the result is a family of functions, not a single one.

label: exemplified by
description: The geometric series is the canonical example of convergence: it converges for |r| < 1 and diverges for |r| ≥ 1, providing the benchmark against which all other convergence tests are calibrated.

label: applies to
description: Convergence is the core property tested for infinite series — a series is defined by whether its partial sums converge to a finite limit, and the radius of convergence specifies where a power series converges.

label: determines range of
description: Each Maclaurin series has a radius of convergence R that determines where it converges: e^x and trig functions converge everywhere (R=∞), while ln(1+x) converges only for |x| < 1.

label: governs validity of
description: A Taylor series is only a valid representation of f(x) within its radius of convergence — outside this interval the series diverges and the polynomial approximation becomes meaningless.

label: tested via
description: Convexity is determined by computing the second derivative f″(x) using differentiation rules — f″ ≥ 0 confirms convexity, f″ ≤ 0 confirms concavity.

label: explains
description: An option’s value is a convex function of the underlying price, which is why gamma (∂²V/∂S²) is positive for long positions — convexity is the mathematical source of the gamma P&L from large price moves.

label: required by
description: Jensen’s inequality is stated in terms of convex and concave functions — understanding convexity is a prerequisite for applying the inequality to expected values in finance.

label: uses
description: The Cosine Rule is built directly on the cosine function — the squared length of one side is expressed as a quadratic function of the cosine of the included angle.

label: combined with
description: When the Cosine Rule gives a quadratic equation in an unknown side, the quadratic formula is applied to solve it — a common two-step technique in triangle calculations.

label: extends
description: The Cosine Rule extends trigonometric analysis beyond right-angled triangles, enabling exact side and angle calculations for any triangle configuration.

label: enables integration of
description: The cover-up rule decomposes rational functions into simple fractions whose indefinite integrals are standard logarithm or power forms, making the technique the essential first step before integrating any rational integrand.

label: applied within
description: Partial fraction decomposition via the cover-up rule is a preparatory step within the broader toolkit of integration rules — once the decomposition is complete, each term is integrated using the standard 1/(x-a) rule.

label: produces integrals involving
description: Integrating the simple fractions produced by the cover-up rule always yields natural logarithm terms, so mastery of logarithmic functions is necessary to evaluate the resulting antiderivatives in closed form.

label: resolves poles via
description: Each linear factor in the denominator corresponds to a simple pole of the rational function; the cover-up rule extracts the residue at that pole, directly connecting partial fraction decomposition to the theory of singularities and residue calculus.

label: enforces continuity via
description: Cubic spline construction enforces continuity of the first and second derivatives at each knot using differentiation rules, which is what produces the smooth curves essential for stable hedging.

label: constructs
description: Cubic spline interpolation is the standard method for constructing the zero rate curve from discrete market quotes, ensuring smooth rates and continuous forward rates between observed tenors.

label: evaluated using
description: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 2 is the practical tool for computing every definite integral — it converts the limit-of-sums definition into the tractable F(b) minus F(a) evaluation, which is how closed-form option pricing formulae are derived.

label: computed using
description: Techniques such as substitution and integration by parts are what make definite integrals tractable — without these rules, integrals over payoff functions or probability densities could not be reduced to closed-form expressions like the Black-Scholes formula.

label: central to
description: European option prices are computed as discounted definite integrals of the payoff function against the risk-neutral density — the Black-Scholes call price is the integral of the payoff times the lognormal density evaluated analytically over the in-the-money region.

label: defines
description: The CDF is a definite integral of the probability density from minus infinity to x — every quantile, probability, and option delta is ultimately a definite integral evaluated at specific limits.

label: requires
description: A differential equation on its own has infinitely many solutions; initial and boundary conditions are the constraints that select the unique, physically meaningful solution — in option pricing, the terminal payoff and domain boundaries transform the Black-Scholes PDE from an abstract equation into a well-posed pricing problem.

label: solved via
description: The integrating factor method is the standard analytical technique for first-order linear ODEs — it is the tool used to solve mean-reverting models such as the Vasicek and Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes that arise from stochastic differential equations in interest-rate and commodity modelling.

label: classified as
description: The Black-Scholes PDE is a second-order linear PDE whose discriminant is zero, placing it in the parabolic class — understanding this classification is the first step in choosing the correct analytical or numerical solution method for an option pricing problem.

label: extended by
description: A stochastic differential equation is the extension of an ordinary differential equation to processes driven by Brownian motion — the SDE for geometric Brownian motion plays the same foundational role in continuous-time finance that simple ODEs play in deterministic growth models.

label: expressed via
description: Leibniz notation d/dx is the most common way to write the differential operator in finance — every term in the Black-Scholes PDE is a partial differential operator expressed in Leibniz form, linking the abstract operator concept directly to the Greeks.

label: formalises
description: The differential operator D makes linearity of differentiation precise: expressing d/dx as a linear map on a function space shows that the additivity and homogeneity properties are not coincidences but consequences of the algebraic structure of the operator, which is why portfolio Greeks add together cleanly.

label: extends to
description: The single-variable differential operator D extends naturally to partial differential operators ∂/∂S and ∂/∂t for functions of several variables — this operator language is used to write the Black-Scholes PDE as a single compact expression LV = 0.

label: generalised by
description: Itô's Lemma is the stochastic analogue of applying the differential operator to a function of a random process — the operator picks up an extra second-order correction term because the quadratic variation of Brownian motion is non-zero, unlike in ordinary calculus.

label: uses
description: Differentiating ln(f(x)) requires the chain rule — the standard result d/dx ln(x) = 1/x is extended to composite arguments by multiplying by f'(x), which is the step needed when differentiating the ln(S/K) term in Black-Scholes with respect to S.

label: extends
description: Differentiating the natural logarithm builds directly on the definition and properties of ln — the result 1/x and the chain-rule extension f'/f are the calculus counterparts of the log's algebraic properties and are what make log-returns tractable.

label: applied in
description: Computing Delta from the Black-Scholes formula requires differentiating ln(S/K) with respect to S, giving 1/S — the rule for differentiating natural logarithms is therefore a direct prerequisite for deriving option Greeks from first principles.

label: verifies
description: Logarithmic differentiation of S_t = S_0 exp((μ - ½σ²)t + σW_t) recovers the GBM stochastic differential equation — applying d/dt ln(S_t) is the standard technique for confirming that the explicit solution satisfies dS = μS dt + σS dW.

label: uses
description: Differentiating composite trigonometric expressions such as sin(ωt + φ) or cos(σ√T) requires the chain rule — the outer trig derivative is multiplied by the derivative of the inner argument, a step that appears whenever frequencies or scaling factors enter the argument.

label: underpins
description: The FFT option-pricing method differentiates the characteristic function — a complex exponential that resolves into sin and cos — with respect to the frequency variable, making trig differentiation a foundational step in deriving Fourier-based pricing formulae.

label: generalises to
description: The derivatives of the reciprocal trig functions sec, csc, and cot are obtained by applying the quotient rule to 1/cos, 1/sin, and cos/sin — they are a direct extension of differentiating the primary trig functions.

label: extends
description: Differentiating trigonometric functions builds directly on knowing what sin, cos, and tan are — the derivative results are themselves members of the same trig family, so fluency with the base functions is a prerequisite.

label: includes
description: The chain rule is one of the core differentiation rules — it handles composite functions and is essential for differentiating any function where one quantity depends on another through an intermediate variable.

label: inverse of
description: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus establishes that differentiation and integration are inverse operations — differentiating the integral of a function recovers the original function, making differentiation rules the mirror image of integration.

label: inverse of
description: Integration is the reverse operation of differentiation — the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus states that the integral of a derivative recovers the original function, and every integration technique has a corresponding differentiation rule it reverses.

label: applied to
description: The derivatives of ln(x) and e^x are among the most important differentiation rules — they appear whenever continuous compounding, log-returns, or the lognormal model are differentiated.

label: generalised by
description: Partial differentiation extends single-variable differentiation to functions of several variables — the same power, product, chain, and quotient rules apply, but each derivative is taken with respect to one variable while holding the others constant.

label: foundation of
description: A Taylor series is built from repeated differentiation — the nth term is the nth derivative evaluated at the expansion point, so mastery of differentiation rules is a prerequisite for constructing any Taylor approximation.

label: applied in
description: The Greeks are partial derivatives of the option price with respect to its inputs — computing Delta, Gamma, Theta, and Vega requires direct application of the chain rule, product rule, and differentiation of the normal CDF.

label: leads to
description: A negative discriminant indicates that the quadratic formula produces complex conjugate roots — the first and most natural entry point to complex numbers at A Level.

label: part of
description: The discriminant b² - 4ac is derived from the quadratic formula — it is the expression under the square root that determines whether the formula produces real or complex solutions.

label: determines
description: The discriminant is the decisive test for whether a quadratic has real roots: positive for two real roots, zero for a repeated real root, negative for no real roots.

label: contrasted with
description: Disproof by counterexample refutes a universal claim with one specific failing instance; proof by contradiction refutes an assumption by deriving a general logical impossibility.

label: negates
description: Proof by induction establishes a universal statement; disproof by counterexample destroys it — finding even one n for which an induction conjecture fails means the conjecture cannot be proved by induction.

label: uses
description: Complex division is performed by multiplying numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator — the conjugate is therefore the operative tool that converts a complex denominator into a real one.

label: extends
description: Division of complex numbers builds directly on the algebra of complex numbers — it applies the conjugate rule and modulus properties introduced in the definition of complex numbers, completing the four arithmetic operations in ℂ.

label: used in
description: The Gil-Pelaez Fourier inversion formula contains integrands of the form e^{-iuk} φ(u) / (iu), which is a division of complex exponentials — understanding modular division and argument subtraction in polar form is essential to evaluate and implement this formula for option pricing.

label: relies on
description: Performing complex division requires fluency with modulus |z| and argument arg(z) notation — the polar form r₁/r₂ e^{i(θ₁−θ₂)} is the most transparent representation of the operation and is expressed entirely in complex-plane notation.

label: constrains
description: Valid composition requires the range of the inner function to be a subset of the domain of the outer function, so domain and range analysis is prerequisite to forming composites.

label: swapped by
description: The domain and range of a function are swapped when forming its inverse: the domain of f⁻¹ is the range of f and vice versa.

label: restricts
description: The logarithm function ln(x) has domain (0,∞) and range (-∞,∞), while the exponential has domain (-∞,∞) and range (0,∞) — they are mutual inverses with swapped domains and ranges.

label: extends
description: Double integration applies the same single-variable rules (power rule, substitution, integration by parts) iteratively across two variables, so mastery of single-variable integration rules is a prerequisite.

label: inverse of
description: By Fubini's theorem, the double integral reverses the process of partial differentiation — just as partial derivatives isolate the rate of change in one variable, iterated integration accumulates along each variable in turn.

label: used in
description: Pricing spread options and basket options requires integrating the joint payoff function over the bivariate risk-neutral density of two correlated assets — a double integral that generally demands numerical quadrature methods.

label: computes probabilities from
description: The probability that a pair of continuous random variables falls in any region is a double integral of their joint density function, making double integration the core tool for working with multivariate distributions in quantitative finance.

label: converts to
description: Euler's formula $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$ is the identity that converts between the polar exponential and Cartesian forms of a complex number, making it the essential tool whenever quants manipulate characteristic functions or complex exponentials in derivative pricing.

label: underpins
description: The Fourier transform kernel $e^{-i\omega x}$ is just Euler's formula in disguise — without the identity $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$ there would be no bridge between complex exponentials and oscillatory decomposition, and hence no characteristic-function approach to option pricing.

label: derived via
description: Euler's formula is proved by expanding $e^{i\theta}$, $\cos\theta$, and $\sin\theta$ as Maclaurin series and matching real and imaginary parts term-by-term — a derivation that reinforces why Taylor series are the backbone of mathematical analysis in quantitative finance.

label: generalises to
description: Replacing the real argument $t$ in the MGF $E[e^{tX}]$ with the imaginary argument $iu$ via Euler's formula yields the characteristic function $E[e^{iuX}]$, which always exists for real $u$ and is therefore preferred in option-pricing models with heavy-tailed log-returns.

label: special case of
description: Euler's identity e^{iπ} + 1 = 0 is the special case θ = π of Euler's formula, showing that the circular functions evaluated at π collapse the imaginary part to zero and give -1, uniting five fundamental constants in one equation.

label: special case of
description: Euler's identity is the special case θ = π of the general Euler's formula e^{iθ} = cos θ + i sin θ — the general formula is required to understand why the identity holds and why the five mathematical constants are connected.

label: extends
description: Euler's identity demonstrates that the real exponential function e^x extends naturally to imaginary arguments, where e^{iπ} = -1 shows that pure imaginary exponents produce rotations rather than growth, underpinning characteristic functions in derivative pricing.

label: motivates
description: The modulus identity |e^{iuX}| = 1 visible in Euler's identity is the reason the characteristic function E[e^{iuX}] always converges even when the moment generating function E[e^{uX}] diverges for fat-tailed distributions, making Euler's identity the conceptual bridge between the two transforms.

label: exemplified by
description: Cosine is the canonical even function: cos(−x) = cos(x) for all x, making it the even component of Euler's formula e^(ix) = cos(x) + i sin(x) and the real part of the characteristic function for symmetric distributions.

label: simplifies
description: The Fourier transform of an even function is purely real — the sine (odd) components integrate to zero — halving the numerical work in characteristic function option pricing for symmetric return distributions.

label: contrasted with
description: Every function on a symmetric domain can be decomposed into an even part (f(x)+f(−x))/2 and an odd part (f(x)−f(−x))/2 — the two symmetry classes are complementary and exhaustive.

label: avoids need for
description: When a function is explicit, differentiation is straightforward; implicit differentiation is only needed when the function cannot be written explicitly — recognising the form determines which technique to apply.

label: contrasted with
description: An explicit function gives y directly as y = f(x); an implicit function gives a relation F(x,y) = 0 that cannot always be solved for y — the distinction determines whether standard differentiation or implicit differentiation is required.

label: enables
description: Factorising the denominator polynomial using the Factor Theorem is the prerequisite step for partial fraction decomposition, which is used to invert Laplace transforms in fixed-income and derivative pricing models.

label: analyses roots of
description: The Factor Theorem establishes which linear terms divide a polynomial exactly, making it the primary tool for determining the root structure of polynomial functions.

label: complements
description: The Factor Theorem is used first to extract rational roots and reduce the polynomial's degree; the quadratic formula is then applied to any remaining degree-2 factor that cannot be factorised by inspection.

label: normalises terms in
description: Factorials appear as the denominators in every Taylor series coefficient — the $n!$ term ensures each successive correction is scaled correctly, which is why the delta–gamma approximation to option prices converges for small moves.

label: expressed using
description: Factorial is the canonical example of product notation: $n! = \prod_{i=1}^{n} i$, so understanding product notation is a prerequisite for reading factorial-based formulae such as binomial coefficients and Taylor series in quantitative finance textbooks.

label: defines coefficient of
description: The binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{k} = n! / (k!(n-k)!)$ is built from factorials and gives the number of paths with exactly $k$ up-moves in a binomial tree, which is the combinatorial heart of binomial option pricing.

label: generalised by
description: The Gamma function generalises the factorial from non-negative integers to all positive real numbers, so every factorial identity is a special case of the Gamma recurrence relation.

label: appears in pmf of
description: The Poisson probability mass function divides $\lambda^k$ by $k!$ to prevent probabilities summing to more than one; this factorial normalisation is also the link between the Poisson distribution and the exponential series $e^\lambda = \sum \lambda^k/k!$, used in jump-diffusion pricing.

label: accelerates
description: The FFT accelerates European option pricing under any model with a known characteristic function, transforming the per-strike pricing problem into a single whole-surface computation.

label: prices options under
description: The FFT prices European options under the Heston model by inverting its known characteristic function, computing prices at all strikes simultaneously in milliseconds.

label: approximates
description: The forward difference approximation is the discrete numerical counterpart of the analytical derivative — it replaces the exact limit definition of f'(x) with a finite ratio that is computable without symbolic differentiation.

label: derived from
description: The forward difference formula is obtained by truncating the Taylor series after the first-order term and rearranging — the Taylor expansion both motivates the formula and reveals that its truncation error is O(h), making it first-order accurate.

label: used in
description: The forward difference formula is one of three standard discretisations used to replace continuous derivatives with grid-based approximations when solving the Black-Scholes PDE numerically; the explicit (FTCS) finite difference scheme uses it for the time derivative.

label: implements
description: Bumping a single input up by h and dividing the price change by h is a direct application of the forward difference formula — it computes Greeks at the cost of one extra pricing call, trading accuracy for computational speed.

label: relies on
description: The Fourier transform kernel e^{-iωx} is a complex exponential — understanding Euler's formula and the algebra of complex numbers is a prerequisite for working with Fourier integrals and interpreting their frequency-domain output.

label: discretised by
description: The FFT is the efficient numerical algorithm for evaluating the discrete approximation to the continuous Fourier transform — the Carr-Madan option pricing method uses this discretisation to invert characteristic functions at speed.

label: underpins
description: The characteristic function of a random variable is precisely its Fourier transform evaluated at real frequencies — mastering the Fourier transform provides the mathematical foundation for understanding why characteristic functions always exist and how to invert them to recover densities and option prices.

label: transforms to/from
description: The Fourier transform of a probability density function yields the characteristic function, and its inverse recovers the density — this duality is the mathematical backbone of FFT-based option pricing under models such as Heston, Variance Gamma, and CGMY.

label: alternative name for
description: The function of a function rule is an alternative name for the chain rule, emphasising the substitution technique — naming the inner function u and differentiating layer by layer — which is the most practical approach when working with composite expressions in option pricing.

label: underpins
description: Integration by substitution is the integral counterpart of the function of a function rule: recognising the inner function u and its derivative in the integrand is what allows a change of variable that reduces a complex integral — such as the Black-Scholes payoff integral — to a standard form.

label: enables change of variable in
description: Evaluating the risk-neutral expectation of an option payoff requires a change of variable that transforms the lognormal integral into a standard normal integral; this change of variable is a direct application of the function of a function rule applied to the exponent of the lognormal density.

label: differentiates kernel of
description: Differentiating the normal density e^(-x²/2) requires the function of a function rule — identifying x² as the inner function immediately gives the derivative -x·φ(x), a result used when computing how Black-Scholes option prices change with strike.

label: convergence guaranteed for
description: The Intermediate Value Theorem guarantees at least one root between two points of opposite sign for a continuous function — the theoretical foundation for all bracketing root-finding methods.

label: found via
description: The Factor Theorem is the primary algebraic technique for finding roots of polynomial functions — testing candidate values and then factorising to find all roots.

label: computed numerically via
description: When algebraic root-finding fails, the Newton-Raphson method iteratively finds roots of functions using the tangent line — the standard numerical method for root-finding in quantitative finance.

label: applied in
description: Part 1 of the FTC gives the key identity F_X’(x) = f_X(x): the derivative of the CDF is the PDF. This relationship is used constantly when converting between cumulative probabilities and densities in option pricing and risk measurement.

label: foundation of
description: Part 2 of the FTC is what makes a PDF useful — the probability P(a < X < b) equals the integral of the density from a to b, evaluated as the antiderivative difference F(b) − F(a).

label: special case of
description: The geometric series is the prototypical convergent infinite series: it converges when |r| < 1 and its closed-form sum 1/(1-r) is the benchmark against which other convergence tests are calibrated.

label: underlies
description: The Taylor series of 1/(1-x) is exactly the geometric series — the Taylor expansion is a power series and for rational functions reduces directly to the geometric sum formula.

label: applied to
description: Graph transformations are applied to base functions (sine, exponential, polynomial) to produce new functions with shifted, stretched, or reflected graphs.

label: related to reflection in
description: The graph of an inverse function f⁻¹ is the reflection of f in the line y = x, making reflection a specific graph transformation that converts a function to its inverse.

label: algebraic form of
description: Linear data coding y = ax + b is the algebraic counterpart of a vertical stretch by a and vertical translation by b, connecting statistical data transformation to geometric function transformation.

label: extends
description: Higher derivatives are produced by applying the differentiation operation repeatedly — understanding the first derivative is an essential prerequisite before considering the curvature, rate-of-curvature, and higher-order sensitivities that the second and third derivatives describe.

label: coefficients of
description: Every term in a Taylor series beyond the linear approximation is a higher derivative — the second-derivative term gives the Gamma correction and the third gives Speed, so higher derivatives are precisely what distinguishes a full Taylor expansion from a simple linear estimate.

label: mathematical basis of
description: Gamma, Speed, and Colour are all higher derivatives of the option price — Gamma is the second derivative with respect to spot, Speed the third, and Colour the mixed third derivative — so the entire hierarchy of higher-order Greeks is an application of repeated differentiation.

label: generalises to
description: Bond convexity is the second derivative of price with respect to yield scaled by price, making it a direct application of the higher-derivative concept to fixed-income — the same mathematical structure (first derivative = duration, second = convexity) appears across all asset classes.

label: extends
description: The gradient collects first-order partial derivatives into a vector; the Hessian extends this by collecting all second-order partial derivatives into a matrix — understanding the gradient is therefore the natural prerequisite before studying the curvature information encoded in the Hessian.

label: builds on
description: Higher-order partial derivatives are obtained by repeatedly applying partial differentiation — computing the Hessian matrix requires mastery of first-order partial derivatives as each entry is itself a partial derivative of a partial derivative.

label: classified by
description: Whether a critical point of a multivariate function is a minimum, maximum, or saddle point is determined by the positive definiteness of the Hessian matrix — linking second-order calculus directly to the linear algebra concept of positive definite matrices.

label: underpins optimisation of
description: Mean-variance portfolio optimisation seeks a minimum of portfolio variance — the second-order conditions guaranteeing that the critical point is indeed a minimum are expressed in terms of the Hessian (the covariance matrix acting as the Hessian of the quadratic objective), connecting higher-order partial derivatives directly to optimal portfolio construction.

label: defined via
description: Hyperbolic functions are defined entirely in terms of the exponential: sinh(x) = (eˣ − e⁻ˣ)/2 and cosh(x) = (eˣ + e⁻ˣ)/2 — they are the hyperbolic analogues of sine and cosine, built from exponential functions.

label: inverted by
description: The inverse hyperbolic functions arcsinh, arccosh, arctanh reverse the hyperbolic functions, just as arcsin/arccos/arctan reverse the circular trig functions — they arise in closed-form integration results.

label: appears in
description: The exact solution for the CIR bond price involves hyperbolic functions — cosh and sinh appear in the closed-form expressions for A(t) and B(t) in the affine term structure formula, arising from the sinh/cosh solutions to the characteristic ODE.

label: applies
description: Implicit differentiation is mechanically an application of the chain rule — every term involving the dependent variable y is differentiated by the chain rule, producing the dy/dx factor that is then isolated algebraically.

label: technique for
description: Implicit differentiation is the standard computational technique for finding derivatives of implicit functions — the Implicit Function Theorem guarantees the derivative exists; implicit differentiation is how you calculate it.

label: used in
description: The Newton-Raphson iteration step is x_{n+1} = x_n - F(x_n)/F'(x_n); when F is an implicit equation such as the Black-Scholes pricing function, F' is computed by implicit differentiation — in particular, the vega used when solving for implied volatility is the implicitly-derived sensitivity of price with respect to sigma.

label: expressed via
description: The implicit differentiation formula dy/dx = -(dF/dx)/(dF/dy) is written entirely in terms of partial derivatives, connecting the single-variable problem of finding dy/dx to the multivariate calculus of partial differentiation.

label: extends
description: Improper integrals extend the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to unbounded domains by replacing the finite limit with a limit process — a generalisation essential for integrating probability densities over the entire real line in continuous finance models.

label: applies
description: Evaluating improper integrals uses the same toolkit of integration rules — substitution, integration by parts, and standard antiderivatives — but wraps the result in a limit, so mastery of integration rules is a prerequisite.

label: used in
description: The expected payoff of a European option is an improper integral of the discounted payoff over the risk-neutral density from the strike to infinity — the Black-Scholes formula is the closed-form evaluation of exactly this improper integral.

label: normalises
description: Every continuous probability density function is normalised by the improper integral over the whole real line equalling one — checking that this integral converges is the same as verifying the distribution is well-defined.

label: inverse of
description: The indefinite integral is the inverse operation of differentiation — finding an antiderivative undoes the derivative, a relationship that underpins every closed-form solution to an ordinary differential equation in quantitative finance.

label: foundation for
description: The indefinite integral (antiderivative) is the concept the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus builds on — Part 2 of the FTC uses an antiderivative to evaluate any definite integral, connecting the two forms of integration.

label: requires
description: An indefinite integral produces a family of solutions differing by an arbitrary constant C; initial or boundary conditions are required to select the unique solution, which is exactly how terminal payoff conditions pin down option pricing PDEs.

label: evaluated using
description: Integration rules — the power rule, substitution, and integration by parts — are the computational toolkit for evaluating indefinite integrals, and mastery of them is a prerequisite for any closed-form pricing or present-value calculation.

label: resolved by
description: Indeterminate forms are precisely the cases where L'Hôpital's Rule applies — the rule converts the unresolvable $\frac{0}{0}$ or $\frac{\infty}{\infty}$ limit into a new limit of derivatives, making the two concepts inseparable in practice.

label: special case of
description: Indeterminate forms arise within limit evaluation — they are the subset of limit problems where substitution fails and the result cannot be read off directly, requiring algebraic transformation or L'Hôpital's Rule to resolve.

label: used to derive
description: The continuous compounding formula $e^r = \lim_{n\to\infty}(1+r/n)^n$ is a $1^\infty$ indeterminate form resolved by taking logarithms and applying L'Hôpital — the exponential function is therefore defined through the resolution of an indeterminate form.

label: related to
description: A removable singularity is the geometric counterpart of a $\frac{0}{0}$ indeterminate form — the function is undefined at the point but the limit exists, and resolving the indeterminate form reveals the value that would make the function continuous there.

label: prerequisite for
description: Differentiating power functions using the rule d/dx(x^n) = nx^{n-1} requires fluent manipulation of indices, including negative and fractional exponents.

label: foundation of
description: Logarithms are defined as the inverse of exponentiation; fluency with index laws is necessary to manipulate exponential and logarithmic expressions in pricing and compounding.

label: generalises
description: Surds are roots written as fractional exponents; index laws provide the rules for manipulating these fractional powers.

label: classified by
description: The discriminant determines whether a quadratic inequality has solutions: a positive discriminant gives two roots bounding the solution region, zero gives a single boundary point, and negative means the quadratic never crosses the axis.

label: solved using
description: Modulus inequalities |f(x)| < a are solved by squaring both sides or using the equivalent double inequality -a < f(x) < a, connecting modulus and inequality techniques.

label: extends
description: Quadratic inequalities are solved by finding the roots of the corresponding quadratic equation, then using a sign table or sketch to identify the solution intervals.

label: generalised by
description: A Taylor series is a specific type of infinite power series — infinite series convergence theory provides the ratio test and radius of convergence that determine where a Taylor series gives a valid approximation.

label: applied in
description: The Black-Scholes PDE is universal across all European options — the terminal condition (payoff) and boundary conditions are what select the unique pricing solution for each specific contract type.

label: determines
description: The terminal condition V(S,T) = max(S-K,0) for a call or max(K-S,0) for a put is the boundary condition that, combined with the Black-Scholes PDE, yields the closed-form European option price.

label: formalised by
description: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus gives the definite integral its computational power — it links the accumulation concept to antiderivatives, turning an area problem into a simple evaluation of F(b) − F(a).

label: computed using
description: Knowing what an integral represents is the foundation; integration rules (power rule, substitution, by parts) provide the techniques for actually evaluating integrals that arise in pricing and probability.

label: defines
description: The CDF is defined as the definite integral of the probability density from minus infinity to x — the accumulation interpretation of the integral is precisely what gives the CDF its meaning as a cumulative probability.

label: generalised by
description: The Itô stochastic integral generalises the Riemann integral to integrands that are random processes — the accumulation concept carries over, but the quadratic variation of Brownian motion requires a modified construction.

label: evaluated via
description: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus is the tool that actually evaluates an integrand over an interval — given the integrand f(x), Part 2 says to find its antiderivative F and compute F(b) − F(a), turning an accumulation problem into arithmetic.

label: simplified by
description: Integration rules (substitution, by-parts, power rule) are techniques for transforming a complicated integrand into a form whose antiderivative can be found — choosing the right technique depends entirely on the structure of the integrand.

label: defines payoff in
description: In risk-neutral pricing, the option price is an expectation integral whose integrand is the discounted contract payoff multiplied by the risk-neutral density — every exotic option differs from a vanilla call solely through a different integrand inside the same risk-neutral expectation.

label: expressed as
description: The expected value of a continuous random variable is a definite integral whose integrand is the product of the outcome value and its probability density — understanding the integrand structure makes it clear why heavier tails raise the expectation.

label: uses
description: The integrating factor method relies on recognising that multiplying by e^{integral P dt} makes the left-hand side an exact derivative, which can then be integrated directly using standard rules.

label: uses
description: The integrating factor is always an exponential function e^{integral P(t) dt}, making properties of the exponential and logarithm essential to the technique.

label: does not solve
description: Unlike the Vasicek model, the CIR model has state-dependent diffusion sigma*sqrt(r) that prevents a simple integrating factor solution — more advanced techniques are needed.

label: solves
description: The integrating factor e^{gamma t} is the standard technique for solving the Vasicek/OU SDE analytically, producing the closed-form solution showing exponentially decaying memory of the initial condition.

label: specialises
description: Integration by parts is one of the core techniques surveyed in the integration rules card — it extends the standard antiderivative table to products of functions by reversing the product rule, and is routinely needed alongside substitution for finance-related integrals.

label: reverses
description: Integration by parts is the integral analogue of the product rule: the formula int u dv = uv - int v du is derived directly by integrating the product rule d(uv) = u dv + v du, so every application of the product rule in differentiation has a mirror in integration by parts.

label: used in derivation of
description: Deriving the Black-Scholes call price involves integrating the payoff max(S-K, 0) against the risk-neutral lognormal density; integration by parts is the step that separates the integral into the two N(d1) and N(d2) terms that appear in the final closed-form formula.

label: used in derivation of
description: The Fokker-Planck equation is derived by integrating by parts twice to transfer the differential operators from the probability density onto smooth test functions, converting the Kolmogorov forward operator into the drift and diffusion terms of the forward equation.

label: reverses
description: Integration by substitution is the reverse of the chain rule — just as the chain rule differentiates a composite function, substitution undoes that composition in an integral, making it indispensable whenever an integrand contains a composed function multiplied by the inner derivative.

label: specialises
description: Substitution is one of the core techniques collected in the integration rules card — it extends the basic antiderivative table by transforming integrals involving composite functions into simpler standard forms.

label: used in
description: Deriving the Black-Scholes call price formula requires integrating the discounted payoff over the risk-neutral lognormal density; the key algebraic step uses substitution to complete the square in the exponent and reduce the integral to the standard normal CDF Phi(d1) and Phi(d2).

label: evaluates integrals of
description: Many probability density functions — such as the normal, lognormal, and chi-squared — cannot be integrated in closed form without substitution to convert the integral into a Gaussian or gamma form; this technique underpins moment calculations and option pricing.

label: applied in
description: Taylor series can be integrated term by term within their radius of convergence, and many integral representations of special functions (such as the error function used in option pricing) are derived by integrating a Taylor expansion.

label: foundation of
description: The CDF is defined as the integral of the probability density function from minus infinity to x — computing any probability from a continuous distribution is a direct application of definite integration.

label: applied in
description: A valid probability density must integrate to one over its support, and every probability calculation involves integrating the PDF over an interval — substitution and by-parts are routinely needed for non-trivial densities.

label: visualised by
description: The graph of an inverse function is found by reflecting in y = x — the graphical counterpart of algebraic inversion that makes domain-range swapping intuitive.

label: exemplified by
description: The logarithm is the inverse of the exponential function — the canonical example used to teach inverse functions, and itself central to continuous-compounding finance.

label: applied in
description: The quantile function Φ⁻¹ is the inverse of the normal CDF and appears directly in Black-Scholes and VaR calculations — the most financially important application of inverse functions.

label: illustrates
description: Reflecting a function's graph in y = x is the geometric realisation of algebraic inversion — the two representations (algebraic formula and graph) are complementary ways of understanding the same inverse relationship.

label: key example of
description: The graphs of y = eˣ and y = ln x are reflections in y = x, making them the standard worked example for inverse graphs and also central to continuous-compounding and log-return calculations in finance.

label: inverse of
description: Inverse hyperbolic functions reverse the hyperbolic functions: arcsinh(x) = ln(x + √(x²+1)), arccosh(x) = ln(x + √(x²−1)) — their logarithmic forms make them expressible via the natural log.

label: expressed via
description: Every inverse hyperbolic function has a closed-form expression involving the natural logarithm — arcsinh(x) = ln(x + √(x²+1)) — making them integrable in closed form and connecting them to log-return calculations.

label: notation for
description: Lagrange's prime notation f'(x), f''(x) is one of the three standard notations for derivatives — compact and unambiguous for single-variable functions, widely used at A Level and in option pricing formulas.

label: extended to
description: Lagrange notation extends naturally to higher derivatives: f'', f''', f⁽⁴⁾ — the prime notation is the standard way to write second and third derivatives in Taylor series coefficients.

label: alternative to
description: Lagrange notation f'(x) and Leibniz notation dy/dx represent the same derivative — Lagrange is more concise for function definitions, while Leibniz is preferred when the rate of change with respect to a specific variable must be explicit.

label: extends
description: The Leibniz integral rule is a generalisation of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus — Part 1 of the FTC is exactly the Leibniz rule in the special case where the lower limit is constant and the integrand does not depend on the parameter.

label: relies on
description: Applying the Leibniz rule requires taking the partial derivative of the integrand with respect to the parameter, making fluency with partial differentiation a prerequisite for differentiating parameter-dependent integrals.

label: used to derive
description: Greeks such as Vega and Rho are obtained by differentiating the option pricing integral with respect to volatility or interest rate — the Leibniz rule justifies moving the derivative inside the expectation integral to obtain closed-form sensitivity expressions.

label: applied in
description: Deriving moment equations from the Fokker-Planck equation requires differentiating integrals of the form ∫ x^n p(x,t) dx with respect to time, a step justified by the Leibniz rule that converts the PDE for the density into ODEs for the moments.

label: used in
description: The Taylor expansion is itself a lemma — a proven result invoked as a building block inside larger proofs, such as deriving the Greeks or the Itô correction term.

label: proves
description: The variance addition lemma for independent random variables — Var(X + Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) — is the foundational step behind the square-root rule for scaling volatility.

label: exemplified by
description: Itô's Lemma is the most important lemma in quantitative finance — a proven stepping-stone result that is invoked every time a function of a stochastic process needs to be differentiated.

label: evaluated by
description: The FTC gives limits practical meaning: the integral from a to b equals F(b) minus F(a).

label: applied with
description: Integration techniques apply within the domain fixed by the limits; substitution must also transform the bounds.

label: defines
description: Moving the upper limit of integration traces the CDF, encoding where probability mass accumulates and determining option prices and VaR quantiles.

label: used in
description: Each probability is a definite integral of the PDF; the bounds set the outcome region, in finance the exercise boundary or loss threshold.

label: stronger than
description: Little-o is a strictly stronger statement than big-O: f = o(g) implies f = O(g) but not vice versa — understanding both notations is necessary to distinguish terms that are merely bounded (big-O) from terms that genuinely vanish (little-o) in asymptotic arguments.

label: used in derivation of
description: The derivation of Ito's lemma discards terms that are o(dt) — such as (ΔS)^2 Δt and higher powers of Δt — and retains only those of order dt, making little-o notation the formal justification for the simplifications that produce the final stochastic differential.

label: example of
description: The logarithm is the inverse of the exponential function — log_b(bˣ) = x — making it the canonical example of an inverse function pair in A Level mathematics.

label: governed by
description: Log laws (product, quotient, and power rules) apply to logarithms of any base — they are the algebraic rules for simplifying log expressions and converting between bases.

label: special case of
description: The natural logarithm ln(x) is the most important logarithm in calculus and finance — it is the logarithm base e and the only logarithm whose derivative is 1/x without a scaling factor.

label: used in
description: Log laws simplify expressions before differentiating — logarithmic differentiation converts products and powers into sums using log laws, making derivatives tractable that would otherwise require repeated product/chain rules.

label: rules for
description: Log laws state the algebraic rules governing logarithms: log(ab) = log a + log b, log(a/b) = log a − log b, log(aⁿ) = n log a — these identities reduce products and powers to sums and multiples.

label: applies to
description: Log laws apply to the natural logarithm ln — the identities ln(ab) = ln a + ln b and ln(aⁿ) = n ln a are used constantly in finance to convert multiplicative log-returns into additive sums.

label: extends
description: Continuous compounding A = Pe^{rt} is the limiting case of discrete compounding — the exponential function provides the natural framework for discounting and present value calculations in continuous time.

label: foundation of
description: The lognormal distribution arises because if ln(S) is normally distributed then S = e^X is lognormal — the entire model rests on the inverse relationship between log and exponential functions.

label: defines
description: Log-returns r_t = ln(S_t/S_{t-1}) are the standard return measure in quantitative finance because the logarithm converts multiplicative price ratios into additive quantities that are easier to model statistically.

label: subject to
description: Each Maclaurin series has a radius of convergence that limits where the expansion is valid — for ln(1+x) this is |x| ≤ 1, which constrains the size of return moves for which log-return approximations are accurate.

label: coefficients involve
description: Every Maclaurin coefficient is f^(n)(0)/n! — the nth derivative at zero divided by n factorial — making the factorial the normalisation constant that controls how quickly higher-order terms shrink.

label: special case of
description: The Maclaurin series is the Taylor series expanded at a = 0 — it is the most commonly used form because most finance approximations are made near zero (small moves, short time steps, small rates).

label: underlies approximation in
description: The GBM log-return ln(S_T/S_0) = (μ - ½σ²)T + σW_T is derived by applying the Maclaurin series of ln(1+x) to the small-increment form of the stock price process.

label: extends
description: Mixed partial derivatives are a specific class of second-order partial derivative — understanding first-order partials and the basic notation is a prerequisite before studying how differentiation with respect to two different variables interacts.

label: computed numerically via
description: Cross-Greeks such as vanna are mixed second-order partial derivatives that typically have no closed-form expression for exotic options — they are computed in practice using a four-point finite difference bump over both spot and volatility.

label: produces cross-Greeks in
description: Vanna and charm are mixed partial derivatives of the option price — vanna measures how delta changes with volatility (and how vega changes with spot), so managing a vol book requires understanding cross-Greeks as mixed partials.

label: underpins risk of
description: The volatility surface introduces a dependence between delta and implied volatility, making vanna (the mixed partial of option price with respect to spot and vol) a key risk measure for managing the P&L of a book hedged against a moving vol surface.

label: leads to
description: Continuous-time financial models (Black-Scholes, interest rate models) are derived by modelling instantaneous rates of change, producing differential equations rather than algebraic relationships.

label: uses
description: Optimisation problems — finding maximum profit or minimum cost — are solved using stationary points; modelling with calculus always culminates in a first-derivative condition.

label: solves equations involving
description: Modulus equations apply the definition of |x| as a piecewise function to split a single modulus equation into two linear cases, each solved separately.

label: algebraic counterpart of
description: Modulus transformations show graphically where the fold occurs; modulus equations find the same critical points algebraically — the two skills are complementary approaches to the same concept.

label: extends
description: Modulus transformations apply the absolute value operation to composed functions y = |f(x)| and f(|x|), extending the basic modulus concept from simple |x| to any curve.

label: produces
description: After a modulus transformation, the result is always a piecewise function with distinct formulas on each side of the fold point — the modulus graph is a piecewise curve.

label: related to
description: Multiplying a complex number by its conjugate gives the square of the modulus — $z\bar{z} = |z|^2$ — which is a direct application of the FOIL multiplication rule and underpins modulus calculations in characteristic-function and Fourier-based option pricing.

label: extends
description: Complex number multiplication is the first non-trivial operation built on top of the complex number system — the FOIL rule and polar product law give operational meaning to the abstract definition $i^2 = -1$ and are prerequisite for working with characteristic functions.

label: feeds into
description: The multiplicative property of characteristic functions for independent random variables — $\varphi_{X+Y}(u) = \varphi_X(u)\varphi_Y(u)$ — is a direct consequence of polar multiplication: moduli multiply and arguments add, mirroring how cumulants add for independent assets in portfolio risk aggregation.

label: uses
description: The multivariate chain rule — $df/dt = \nabla f \cdot \mathbf{x}'(t)$ — is the mechanism by which the total derivative propagates changes along a path; it is indispensable when differentiating composite functions of several correlated assets.

label: introduces
description: The gradient is the natural vector generalisation of the derivative to multiple dimensions — multivariate calculus defines $\nabla f$ as the vector of partial derivatives and interprets it geometrically as the direction of steepest ascent, which underpins gradient-descent calibration in quantitative finance.

label: foundation of
description: Partial derivatives are the building blocks of multivariate calculus — every concept in multivariable differentiation (total derivative, gradient, Jacobian, Hessian) is constructed from partial derivatives, so mastering them is the prerequisite for any multi-variable finance model.

label: prerequisite for
description: The multi-asset Ito's lemma applies the multivariate chain rule to stochastic functions of several correlated underlyings — the total-derivative structure dV = sum_i (partial V / partial S_i) dS_i is exactly the multivariate calculus total derivative extended to the stochastic setting.

label: defines
