description: The Newton quotient (f(x+h) − f(x))/h is the formal definition of the derivative — the derivative f'(x) is the limit of this ratio as h → 0, providing the rigorous foundation for all differentiation rules.

label: yields
description: Taking the limit of the Newton quotient produces the derivative f'(x) in Lagrange notation — the prime notation is shorthand for the full limit definition via the Newton quotient.

label: requires
description: The derivative is defined as the limit of the Newton quotient as h → 0 — without the concept of a limit, the Newton quotient is just a difference ratio, not a derivative.

label: requires
description: Newton-Raphson requires the derivative of the function at each iteration — for implied volatility this is vega, computed via differentiation of the Black-Scholes formula.

label: uses vega from
description: The Newton-Raphson implied volatility solver uses vega (the option price sensitivity to volatility) as the derivative in each iteration step, making vega essential to the algorithm s convergence.

label: solves for
description: Newton-Raphson is the standard algorithm for computing implied volatility — it iteratively inverts the Black-Scholes formula using vega as the derivative to converge on the volatility matching the observed option price.

label: extends
description: The nth derivative is the natural generalisation of the first derivative — repeated differentiation builds on the same limit definition, so fluency with first-principle differentiation is a prerequisite for working with higher-order derivatives.

label: defines coefficients of
description: Every coefficient in a Taylor series is determined by an nth derivative evaluated at the expansion point — the nth term is f^(n)(a)/n!, so computing a Taylor series requires computing nth derivatives systematically.

label: generalises
description: The Greeks form a hierarchy of nth derivatives of the option price: Delta is the first derivative with respect to S, Gamma the second, and Speed the third — understanding nth derivatives as a unified concept clarifies how higher-order Greeks extend the standard Greeks framework.

label: gives rise to
description: Speed is defined as the third derivative of the option price with respect to the underlying price, making it an application of the nth derivative concept — it measures how Gamma itself changes as S moves and matters for the accuracy of Gamma hedges.

label: contrasted with
description: Odd functions satisfy f(−x) = −f(x) while even functions satisfy f(−x) = f(x) — every function can be decomposed into its odd and even parts, and the two symmetry classes determine the imaginary and real parts of characteristic functions.

label: simplifies
description: The Fourier transform of an odd function is purely imaginary — the cosine (even) components integrate to zero — and for symmetric probability densities the characteristic function's imaginary part vanishes because the density is even, not odd.

label: exemplified by
description: Sine is the canonical odd function: sin(−x) = −sin(x) for all x, making it the imaginary part of e^(ix) and responsible for the antisymmetric components of Fourier integrals.

label: specialises
description: The ordinary derivative is the single-variable instance of differentiation — framing it explicitly as 'ordinary' (using straight d) prepares students to recognise the contrast with partial derivatives when they encounter multivariable finance functions.

label: expressed in
description: The dy/dx notation that defines the ordinary derivative is Leibniz notation — understanding the straight-d form as distinct from the curly-∂ of partial derivatives is one of the most practically important distinctions in quantitative finance.

label: building block of
description: An ordinary differential equation is built from ordinary derivatives — the ODE dV/dt = rV that underpins continuous compounding is only well-defined because V depends on the single variable t, making dy/dx the correct notation rather than ∂V/∂t.

label: contrasted with
description: The ordinary derivative dy/dx applies when a function has one variable; the partial derivative ∂f/∂x applies when several variables are present — this distinction separates bond duration analysis (ODE) from option pricing via the Black-Scholes PDE (partial derivatives).

label: solved by
description: The integrating factor method is the standard analytical technique for solving first-order linear ODEs — which include the Vasicek mean-reversion equation — making it the direct next step after understanding what an ODE is.

label: generalised by
description: A PDE is the multi-variable generalisation of an ODE — the Black-Scholes PDE involves partial derivatives in both time and the asset price, whereas an ODE for a bank account involves only a single time derivative.

label: derives
description: The continuous compounding formula V(t) = V0 e^{rt} is the solution to the ODE dV/dt = rV — so every present and future value calculation in derivatives pricing rests on solving this simple first-order ODE.

label: extended to
description: An SDE is an ODE with a random noise term added — understanding the deterministic ODE skeleton (obtained by setting the diffusion coefficient to zero) is the first step towards interpreting and solving stochastic models like GBM and Vasicek.

label: example of
description: An upward-opening parabola (a > 0) is a convex function — its second derivative 2a > 0 everywhere, making the vertex a global minimum and all chords above the curve.

label: graph of
description: A parabola is the graph of a quadratic equation y = ax² + bx + c — the visual representation of the quadratic function, showing its roots (x-intercepts), vertex (minimum or maximum), and axis of symmetry.

label: has vertex at
description: The vertex of a parabola is its unique stationary point — a minimum when a > 0 (convex parabola) or a maximum when a < 0 — found by setting the derivative 2ax + b = 0.

label: classifies
description: The Black-Scholes PDE is a parabolic partial differential equation — its discriminant B^2 - 4AC = 0 because there is no u_yy term. The parabolic classification guarantees that option prices are smooth even when payoffs have kinks.

label: classifies
description: The Fokker-Planck (Kolmogorov forward) equation is a parabolic PDE — it describes the time evolution of transition probability densities for diffusion processes, and its parabolic nature ensures these densities smooth out over time.

label: applies
description: Parametric differentiation is an application of the chain rule: dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt) is derived by writing dy/dx = (dy/dt) × (dt/dx) and recognising dt/dx = 1/(dx/dt).

label: differentiates
description: Parametric differentiation is the calculus extension of parametric equations — once a curve is defined parametrically, this technique finds its gradient without first eliminating the parameter.

label: generalised by
description: Itô's lemma is the stochastic generalisation of parametric differentiation — it computes the total differential of a function of a stochastic process using the same chain rule structure, with an extra second-order term arising from Brownian quadratic variation.

label: differentiated via
description: Parametric differentiation is the technique for finding gradients and rates of change for curves defined parametrically — the natural next step after introducing parametric equations.

label: often uses
description: Trigonometric functions are the standard parametrisation of circles and ellipses: x = r cos t, y = r sin t traces a circle — the most commonly encountered parametric curve.

label: prerequisite for
description: Initial and boundary conditions constrain solutions to PDEs, which are equations expressed in terms of partial derivatives — understanding partial differentiation is essential before specifying or interpreting these constraints.

label: foundation of
description: Parabolic PDEs are second-order partial differential equations — classifying and solving them requires fluency in partial derivatives, including the second-order terms u_xx, u_xy, and u_yy that enter the discriminant.

label: used in
description: The similarity solution method relies on computing partial derivatives of the ansatz p = t^a f(xi) with respect to both t and y using the chain rule for composite functions — partial differentiation is the mechanical core of the technique.

label: foundation of
description: The Black-Scholes PDE is expressed entirely in terms of partial derivatives of the option price with respect to the underlying price, time, and volatility — without partial differentiation the equation cannot be stated or solved.

label: applied in
description: Each Greek is a partial derivative of the option price: Delta = dV/dS, Gamma = d2V/dS2, Theta = dV/dt, Vega = dV/dsigma — computing and interpreting them requires fluency in partial differentiation.

label: uses
description: Computing the partial derivative of a composite finance function — such as differentiating the Black-Scholes price with respect to S, where d₁ is itself a function of S — requires applying the chain rule within the partial differentiation.

label: applies
description: Partial differentiation is the practical technique for computing partial derivatives — the concept card defines what a partial derivative is, while this card covers the mechanics of applying the product rule, chain rule, and implicit differentiation to multivariate finance functions.

label: uses
description: The product rule is essential when partially differentiating composite products such as S·N(d₁) in the Black-Scholes formula — it must be combined with the chain rule because d₁ is also a function of S.

label: computes
description: The Greeks — Delta, Gamma, Theta, Vega — are computed by applying partial differentiation to the option pricing function; the Delta derivation for Black-Scholes is a worked example requiring the product rule, chain rule, and a cancellation identity.

label: paired with
description: Partial fractions and completing the square are the two core algebraic pre-processing steps for integrating rational functions — in finance they jointly enable closed-form solutions to pricing equations involving quadratic denominators such as those arising in affine term-structure models.

label: enables
description: Partial fraction decomposition is used to invert Laplace and Fourier transforms analytically — the Black-Scholes PDE solution via the heat equation transformation relies on this to recover the option price in closed form.

label: alternative to
description: Partial fractions and integration by parts are complementary techniques for integrating rational and product-form integrands — together they cover the majority of closed-form integrals encountered when pricing derivatives or computing expected values analytically.

label: simplifies to
description: Partial fraction decomposition reduces a complex rational integrand into a sum of simple fractions, each of which is handled directly by the standard logarithm and power integration rules — making it a prerequisite step before applying those rules in quant finance derivations.

label: displays
description: Pascal's triangle is a visual display of all binomial coefficients, with row n giving C(n,0) through C(n,n) — it makes the symmetry, Pascal's identity, and row sums immediately visible.

label: gives coefficients for
description: Reading off row n of Pascal's triangle immediately gives the coefficients of (a+b)^n — the triangle eliminates all computation when expanding low-degree binomials.

label: structure mirrors
description: The branching structure of the CRR binomial option pricing tree mirrors Pascal's triangle: at step n there are n+1 nodes, and the number of paths reaching each node is the corresponding entry in row n of the triangle.

label: boundary of
description: A point of inflexion is where a function transitions between convex (f'' > 0) and concave (f'' < 0) regions — it marks the boundary of convexity, which is the key concept in Jensen's inequality and option pricing.

label: identified via
description: Points of inflexion require the second derivative to equal zero and change sign — they are defined using second-order information about the function.

label: distinct from
description: Stationary points require f'(x) = 0; points of inflexion require f''(x) = 0 with sign change. A stationary point of inflexion satisfies both simultaneously — it is neither a maximum nor a minimum.

label: represents
description: Every complex number has a unique polar representation $re^{i\theta}$; polar co-ordinates provide the geometric language — modulus and argument — that makes multiplication, division, and powers of complex numbers algebraically simple, which is essential when working with characteristic functions in quantitative finance.

label: used in
description: The Carr-Madan FFT option-pricing method evaluates the characteristic function $\varphi(u) = r(u)\,e^{i\theta(u)}$ at many frequencies simultaneously; the polar form separates the amplitude $r(u)$ from the phase $\theta(u)$, which is the natural decomposition when assessing the numerical stability of the FFT inversion.

label: foundation of
description: The Fourier transform kernel $e^{-i\omega x}$ is a unit-modulus complex exponential — a point on the unit circle in polar co-ordinates — so fluency with the polar plane is a prerequisite for understanding how the transform decomposes functions into frequency components used in option pricing.

label: expressed via
description: Converting between polar and Cartesian co-ordinates requires $x = r\cos\theta$ and $y = r\sin\theta$, making trigonometric functions the algebraic bridge between the two representations; this conversion is performed explicitly every time a complex exponential is written as $\cos\theta + i\sin\theta$ in Fourier analysis.

label: related to
description: The polar form z = r·e^{iθ} makes the conjugate immediately visible as z̄ = r·e^{-iθ} — conjugation simply negates the argument, which is the geometric reflection across the real axis exploited in the conjugate-symmetry property of characteristic functions.

label: represents
description: Polar form is the exponential representation of a complex number — it extends the Cartesian definition by expressing z via its modulus and argument, making multiplication and exponentiation far simpler and underpinning the characteristic-function machinery used in FFT option pricing.

label: used in
description: The FFT algorithm computes the discrete Fourier transform by recursively combining polar-form complex exponentials — in the Carr-Madan option-pricing method the characteristic function evaluations are polar-form numbers whose moduli and arguments are combined at each butterfly step.

label: basis of
description: The Fourier transform kernel e^{-iωx} is a complex exponential in polar form with unit modulus — the decomposition of a function into frequencies is geometrically the projection onto the unit circle, so polar arithmetic is the natural language for working with Fourier integrals.

label: reveals
description: The quotient from dividing a rational function gives the oblique or horizontal asymptote — the polynomial part that the function approaches as x → ±∞.

label: prerequisite for
description: When the degree of the numerator exceeds that of the denominator in a rational function, polynomial long division must be applied first before partial fraction decomposition.

label: confirmed by
description: The remainder from polynomial long division equals f(a) by the remainder theorem — a quick way to verify the long division has been performed correctly.

label: combined with
description: The product and chain rules are frequently applied together — differentiating a composite product such as S·N(d₁) in the Black-Scholes formula requires the chain rule inside each factor and the product rule to combine them.

label: component of
description: The product rule is one of the four core differentiation rules alongside the sum, chain, and quotient rules — it is listed as (fg)′ = f′g + fg′ and is a prerequisite for differentiating any expression that is a product of two non-constant terms.

label: motivates
description: Integration by parts — the most important technique for integrating products of functions — is derived directly from the product rule by integrating both sides of (uv)′ = u′v + uv′, making the product rule the foundation of the integration-by-parts formula.

label: used to derive
description: Delta, the first derivative of the option price with respect to the underlying, is obtained by applying the product rule to S·N(d₁) in the Black-Scholes call formula — without the product rule the Delta expression cannot be correctly derived.

label: contrasted with
description: Proof by contradiction proves a statement true by deriving a contradiction from its negation; disproof by counterexample proves a statement false by finding one specific failing case — opposite logical directions.

label: alternative to
description: Proof by contradiction and proof by induction are the two main proof strategies in A Level mathematics: induction proves statements about all positive integers, contradiction proves existence and impossibility results.

label: proves irrationality of
description: The irrationality of √2, π, and e are all established by proof by contradiction — assuming rationality leads to a logical contradiction, proving the numbers cannot be expressed as p/q.

label: power law case of
description: Power-law proportionality y = kxⁿ is a monomial (single-term polynomial), linking proportionality to polynomial functions at the special case of one term.

label: describes tail of
description: The Pareto tail P(X > x) = (x_m/x)^α is a power-law proportionality P ∝ x^{-α}, making proportionality the algebraic language for describing Pareto-distributed loss severities.

label: expressed as
description: The square-root time rule σ_T = σ√T is a direct proportionality with n = 1/2, so volatility scaling with horizon is a power-law proportional relationship.

label: derived via
description: The quadratic formula is derived by completing the square on the general quadratic ax² + bx + c = 0.

label: produces
description: The quadratic formula yields roots containing √(b²−4ac), which are surds whenever the discriminant is not a perfect square.

label: used in
description: Finding eigenvalues of a 2×2 matrix requires solving the characteristic equation, which is a quadratic — the quadratic formula gives the eigenvalues directly.

label: component of
description: The quotient rule is one of the four core differentiation rules alongside the sum, chain, and product rules — it handles any expression of the form f/g and is essential for differentiating ratios that appear throughout finance.

label: derived from
description: The quotient rule can be derived from the product rule by rewriting f/g as f·g⁻¹ and applying the chain rule to g⁻¹, showing that the quotient rule is not an independent axiom but a consequence of more fundamental differentiation rules.

label: used to derive
description: Several of the Greeks require the quotient rule when differentiating ratio-based expressions — for example, differentiating the Sharpe ratio with respect to volatility or deriving the sensitivity of a hedge ratio to the underlying price.

label: underpins
description: Modified duration is computed by differentiating bond price with respect to yield; since bond price is expressed as a ratio of discounted cash flows, the quotient rule is needed to obtain the correct expression and sign of the duration formula.

label: unit used in
description: Fourier transforms use angular frequency ω measured in radians per unit time; the radian measure of angle is essential for the sinusoidal basis functions e^(iωx) to integrate correctly over all space.

label: required by
description: Trigonometric differentiation formulas (d/dx sin x = cos x) are only valid when angles are measured in radians — the radian is the natural unit of angle for calculus.

label: relies on
description: Rationalisation uses index laws (especially a^{1/2} × a^{1/2} = a) to eliminate roots — the difference-of-squares identity is an application of exponent arithmetic.

label: applies to
description: Rationalisation is the primary technique for eliminating surds from denominators, making it the direct practical companion to surd theory.

label: simplifies
description: Rationalising expressions containing σ√T is a routine step when deriving and simplifying Black-Scholes Greeks and closed-form option pricing results.

label: generates
description: An arithmetic sequence is defined by the first-order linear recurrence u_{n+1} = u_n + d, making recurrence relations the recursive definition that arithmetic series satisfy.

label: analysed via
description: Whether a recurrence relation converges to a fixed point is determined by the convergence condition |f'(L)| < 1 at the fixed point, linking difference equations to limit analysis.

label: generates
description: A geometric sequence is defined by the recurrence u_{n+1} = r·u_n, so geometric series are a special case of first-order linear recurrence relations with zero constant term.

label: structure of
description: GARCH is a recurrence relation for conditional variance σ²_{t+1} = ω + αε²_t + βσ²_t — the fixed-point convergence condition α+β < 1 ensures stationarity and mean-reversion of volatility.

label: technique for
description: The exponential substitution u = e^{αx+βt} v reduces any constant-coefficient linear parabolic PDE to the heat equation — exploiting the fact that the heat equation has known analytical solutions.

label: complements
description: Once reduced to the heat equation, the solution is a convolution with the Gaussian kernel — the same self-similar structure studied in similarity solutions, connecting the substitution technique to the fundamental solution.

label: solves
description: The Black–Scholes PDE is reduced to the heat equation via a change of variables and exponential substitution — this is how the closed-form Black–Scholes formula is derived, producing the N(d₁) and N(d₂) terms.

label: derived via
description: Every reduction formula for integrals of the form int x^n e^{-x} dx or int sin^n x dx is obtained by choosing u and dv strategically in integration by parts, so integration by parts is the underlying engine that generates the recursive identity.

label: extends
description: A reduction formula is an advanced integration technique that recursively lowers the degree of an integrand; it extends the standard toolkit of integration rules to handle families of integrals that would otherwise require repeated manual application of by-parts.

label: evaluates moments of
description: The moments of the chi-squared distribution are computed using the gamma reduction formula int_0^infty x^n e^{-x} dx = n!; these moments determine the mean and variance of the sum of squared normal variables used in the Jarque-Bera normality test applied to financial returns.

label: supports computation of
description: The Jarque-Bera test statistic involves skewness and kurtosis, whose population values under normality follow chi-squared distributions; evaluating the exact moments of these distributions uses the gamma reduction formula, connecting recursive integration to goodness-of-fit testing in finance.

label: generalises
description: The factor theorem is the special case of the remainder theorem where the remainder is zero — if f(a) = 0 then (x-a) is a factor, making the remainder theorem the more general result.

label: evaluates
description: The remainder theorem evaluates the polynomial at a specific point as a by-product of division, connecting polynomial evaluation to the algebraic structure of polynomial divisibility.

label: provides shortcut for
description: The remainder theorem gives the remainder f(a) without performing polynomial long division, making it a quick check that can confirm or replace the full division algorithm.

label: extends
description: The cover-up rule gives a direct formula for the constant at the highest power of a repeated factor, but the lower-power constants require extra steps — so the repeated-factor technique extends the cover-up rule to handle higher-order poles in pricing and transform inversion problems.

label: reduces to
description: After decomposing into repeated-factor terms, each term of the form $A_k/(x-a)^k$ integrates using the negative-power rule, converting an apparently intractable rational integrand into a sum of standard power and logarithm integrals used throughout quantitative finance derivations.

label: special case of
description: A repeated linear factor is a special case that requires additional terms in the partial fraction decomposition — one term per power — making it the key generalisation needed when the denominator polynomial has a degenerate root, as arises in certain Laplace-transform inversions in derivative pricing.

label: creates
description: A repeated linear factor $(x-a)^n$ in the denominator creates a pole of order $n$ at $x = a$ — a higher-order singularity that contributes multiple terms in the Laurent expansion and correspondingly multiple terms in the partial fraction decomposition.

label: computes
description: The reverse chain rule produces the antiderivative of a composite function when the integrand contains a function and its inner derivative — the most common pattern in closed-form integration.

label: reverses
description: The reverse chain rule directly undoes the chain rule — recognising f'(g(x))·g'(x) as the derivative of f(g(x)) allows the antiderivative to be written by inspection.

label: simplified form of
description: The reverse chain rule is integration by substitution carried out mentally — when the substitution u = g(x) is obvious, the du = g'(x) dx step is performed by inspection rather than written out explicitly.

label: relies on
description: Richardson extrapolation requires knowing the order p of the leading error term — expressed using big-O notation as O(h^p) — so that the cancellation step uses the correct weighting factor 2^p.

label: accelerates
description: Richardson extrapolation can be applied to a finite-difference PDE solver by running the grid at two resolutions and combining the results, cancelling the leading error term and achieving fourth-order accuracy from a second-order scheme at little extra cost.

label: related to
description: Rotational symmetry about the origin (f(−x, −y) = f(x, y)) generalises the odd/even function concept to two dimensions — a function with rotational symmetry has even behaviour in radial distance, relevant in 2D probability densities.

label: property of
description: The bivariate standard normal density is rotationally symmetric — its contours are circles — which is why the standard normal can be factored as a product of two independent univariate normals and why Brownian motion in 2D is isotropic.

label: measured by
description: Sensitivity of a quantity to an input is the partial derivative of that quantity with respect to the input — the mathematical formalisation of 'how much does the output change per unit change in this input, holding all others fixed'.

label: applied as
description: Option Greeks are sensitivities in the technical sense: delta is ∂V/∂S, gamma is ∂²V/∂S², theta is ∂V/∂t — each Greek is the partial derivative of the option price with respect to one market parameter.

label: derives
description: The similarity exponent b = 1/2 that emerges from dimensional consistency is exactly the square-root-of-time scaling — volatility scales as sigma*sqrt(T) because the underlying diffusion equation is self-similar with this exponent.

label: solves
description: Applying the similarity ansatz to the Fokker-Planck equation for free Brownian motion yields the Gaussian transition density — the fundamental solution that underpins all diffusion-based pricing models.

label: may reduce to
description: Solving a system of one linear and one quadratic equation by substitution produces a quadratic equation — finding its roots gives the coordinates of the intersection points of the two curves.

label: extended by
description: A-level simultaneous equations are the 2×2 case of systems of linear equations — the general n×n case uses Gaussian elimination and matrix methods covered in linear algebra.

label: paired with
description: The Sine Rule and Cosine Rule are complementary triangle solvers: the Sine Rule is used when two angles and one side are known, while the Cosine Rule is used when two sides and the included angle are known.

label: uses
description: The Sine Rule expresses the proportionality between each side of a triangle and the sine of its opposite angle, making it a direct application of the sine function to general triangles.

label: applies
description: The Sine Rule applies trigonometric functions to oblique triangles, providing the exact proportional relationship between sides and angles once any one side-angle pair is known.

label: violates
description: A singularity is precisely a point where continuity breaks down — understanding singularities clarifies the boundary between well-behaved functions and those requiring special numerical treatment in option pricing.

label: characterised by
description: Singularities are identified and classified through limiting behaviour — a pole is where the limit diverges to infinity, a removable singularity is where the limit exists but the function is undefined, linking singularity theory directly to limit analysis.

label: arises in
description: The Black-Scholes PDE, a parabolic PDE, has a degenerate singularity at S = 0 where the diffusion coefficient vanishes — this boundary singularity must be handled analytically rather than numerically when solving the PDE on a finite grid.

label: constrains boundary of
description: The singularity of the Black-Scholes PDE at S = 0 forces finite-difference pricers to apply the call boundary condition V(0, t) = 0 analytically, rather than evaluating the degenerate PDE at that point.

label: characterises
description: A local minimum of a convex function is also a global minimum, and the second derivative being positive at a stationary point is both the second-order condition and the local convexity criterion.

label: found via
description: Stationary points are located by setting the first derivative equal to zero — the fundamental application of differentiation to optimisation.

label: classified via
description: The second derivative test classifies stationary points as maxima, minima, or inconclusive — the primary application of higher derivatives in A Level calculus.

label: generalised by
description: Surds are roots expressed as fractional exponents (a^{1/n}), and logarithmic and exponential functions generalise this relationship — understanding surd manipulation is the algebraic prerequisite for working fluently with exponential and log expressions in pricing formulae.

label: appears in
description: The Black-Scholes formula contains the term σ√T in the definitions of d₁ and d₂ — simplifying and manipulating this surd expression correctly is essential when computing option prices and sensitivities by hand.

label: foundation of
description: The square-root rule for scaling volatility (σ_annual = σ_daily × √252) is a direct application of surd arithmetic — understanding how to simplify and combine square roots is necessary to apply the rule correctly across different time horizons.

label: exhibits
description: The tangent function has vertical asymptotes at x = π/2 + nπ (where cos x = 0) — it is the standard A Level example of a function with periodic asymptotes, making it the first encounter with asymptotic behaviour in trigonometry.

label: defined from
description: tan(x) = sin(x)/cos(x) — the tangent function is a ratio of the other two primary trigonometric functions, so its properties (period, zeros, asymptotes) follow directly from those of sine and cosine.

label: one of
description: Tangent is one of the three primary trigonometric functions alongside sine and cosine — defined as tan(x) = sin(x)/cos(x), it has period π and vertical asymptotes wherever cos(x) = 0.

label: uses
description: The gradient of the tangent at a point equals the derivative f'(x) at that point — tangent lines are the geometric interpretation of the derivative.

label: produces
description: The tangent and normal at any point are straight lines, and their equations are found using the point-slope form of a linear equation y - y₁ = m(x - x₁).

label: underlies
description: Delta hedging is the financial application of the tangent line: the delta Δ = ∂V/∂S is the gradient of the option price curve, and holding a delta-matched portfolio is approximating the curved payoff by its tangent.

label: applied in
description: The continuous-time limit derivation uses a Taylor expansion of the option value in powers of delta-t to extract the Black-Scholes PDE from the discrete binomial pricing formula.

label: foundation of
description: The Delta-Gamma approximation is a second-order Taylor expansion of the option price around the current underlying price — it directly applies the Taylor series truncated at the quadratic term.

label: motivates
description: Itô’s Lemma is the stochastic calculus equivalent of a Taylor expansion — it tells you how a function of a random process changes, using the same first- and second-derivative structure. The proof of Itô’s Lemma proceeds by Taylor-expanding f(S, t) to second order and substituting (dW)² = dt — the Taylor expansion is not merely motivational but the actual derivation technique.

label: solved using
description: The principal value solution to a trigonometric equation sin θ = k is found using arcsin(k); the general solution adds multiples of the period using the symmetry of the sine curve.

label: involves
description: Trigonometric equations are equations in the trigonometric functions sin, cos, and tan — understanding the graphs and periodicity of these functions is prerequisite to finding all solutions.

label: basis of
description: The Fourier transform decomposes any signal into sinusoidal components — each component is a transformed trigonometric graph characterised by amplitude, frequency, and phase, the same parameters used to describe sinusoidal graphs at A Level.

label: exemplifies
description: Sin and cos are the canonical periodic functions; their graphs define what periodicity looks like, with period 2π, and any transformation of these graphs preserves periodicity with a modified period.

label: visualises
description: Trigonometric graphs are the visual representation of the sine, cosine, and tangent functions — amplitude, period, and phase shift are read off the graph and matched to the algebraic parameters.

label: proved using
description: The derivatives of sin and cos are derived from first principles using the compound angle identities sin(x+h) = sin x cos h + cos x sin h in the limit as h → 0.

label: enables
description: Double angle and Pythagorean identities convert integrals of powers of trig functions (e.g. sin²x) into integrable first-order forms — the standard technique for integration of trig powers.

label: relates
description: Trigonometric identities express exact algebraic relationships between sin, cos, tan and their reciprocals — they are the tools for rewriting trig expressions without changing their value.

label: angle formula mirrors
description: The formula cos θ = (u·v)/(|u||v|) for the angle between vectors is the vector form of the Cosine Rule — both express how a cosine encodes the relative orientation of two directed quantities.

label: line equations use
description: Vector equations of lines r = a + λb in 3D are manipulated by solving simultaneous equations to find intersection points or distances between lines.

label: foundation of
description: A-level vectors introduce the dot product and magnitude in 2D/3D; the dot-product-and-norms card extends these to n-dimensional spaces and connects them to the matrix inner product used in portfolio analysis.

label: implements
description: This card translates the closed-form Black-Scholes equation into vectorised Python code — the mathematical formula for d₁, d₂, and the call/put prices maps directly to NumPy and SciPy operations, making the implementation a line-by-line mirror of the theory.

label: computes
description: The Python implementation computes delta as norm.cdf(d₁) for calls and norm.cdf(d₁) − 1 for puts — the Example section demonstrates this across a grid of spot prices, showing how delta varies from deep out-of-the-money to deep in-the-money.

label: extends to
description: The vectorised Black-Scholes implementation naturally extends to computing Greeks — delta is computed analytically via N(d₁), and other Greeks follow from the same d₁/d₂ building blocks or by finite difference on the pricing function.

label: type of
description: Decision trees are a class of supervised ML model — interpretable and fast to train, but prone to overfitting when grown deep, which motivates ensemble methods.

label: aggregated into
description: A random forest is an ensemble of many individual decision trees — each tree is trained on a bootstrap sample with random feature subsets, and their predictions are averaged to reduce variance.

label: contrasted with
description: Naive Bayes classifiers and decision trees are both interpretable classification models used in credit scoring — Bayes assumes feature independence while trees model arbitrary interactions, making trees more flexible but also more prone to overfitting.

label: compared with
description: Newton-Raphson converges in far fewer iterations but requires the Hessian (second derivatives) which is expensive in high dimensions; gradient descent uses only first derivatives and scales to millions of parameters.

label: computes gradient via
description: The gradient ∇L used in each update step is a vector of partial derivatives of the loss with respect to each parameter — gradient descent is partial differentiation applied iteratively.

label: seeks
description: Gradient descent converges to a stationary point (∇L = 0) of the loss function — the same first-order condition used to find minima analytically, but solved iteratively when a closed form is unavailable.

label: trains
description: Gradient descent is the standard algorithm for minimising the loss function during ML model training — without it, models with more than a few parameters cannot be fitted efficiently.

label: applied in
description: ML models are used to estimate expected returns and covariance matrices as inputs to portfolio optimisation — replacing sample estimates with regularised, data-driven forecasts improves out-of-sample Sharpe ratios.

label: formalised by
description: Bayesian ML treats model parameters as random variables and updates beliefs using Bayes' theorem — the posterior over weights given data is proportional to the likelihood times the prior, unifying regularisation (the prior) with parameter estimation.

label: generalises
description: OLS regression (beta estimation) is the simplest supervised ML model — minimising the same mean squared error loss function; ML extends this to non-linear models, regularisation, and high-dimensional feature spaces.

label: uses in feature selection
description: Feature selection in ML models uses correlation analysis to identify and remove highly collinear inputs, preventing multicollinearity from distorting model weights — exactly the same concern as in factor model construction.

label: implements
description: This card implements the Monte Carlo simulation concept in Python using NumPy's vectorised operations — the theory of averaging random payoffs is the same, but the implementation replaces path-by-path loops with matrix algebra for orders-of-magnitude speedup.

label: applies
description: The vectorised time-stepping scheme is the Euler-Maruyama discretisation applied to the log-price process — the exp(·) wrapper converts the additive increments back to multiplicative GBM steps.

label: simulates
description: The Python simulation discretises the GBM SDE into log-normal increments — each column of the random matrix is one realisation of the geometric Brownian motion path from t=0 to T.

label: computes inputs for
description: The delta-gamma approximation uses delta and gamma to estimate P&L for a small move in the underlying — numerical finite differences are the universal method for computing those delta and gamma values when no closed-form expression exists.

label: implements
description: This card provides the Python implementation of the finite-difference Greek formulas — the central-difference delta and gamma approximations — turning the mathematical definitions into executable code.

label: numerically computes
description: Option Greeks are partial derivatives of the option price with respect to model inputs — numerical finite differences approximate these derivatives by bumping each input and measuring the price change, bypassing the need for analytical formulae.

label: accelerates
description: Monte Carlo simulation generates thousands or millions of price paths and aggregates statistics across them — vectorised NumPy operations replace per-path Python loops, making the difference between a simulation that runs in seconds and one that takes hours.

label: enables efficient computation of
description: Computing means, variances, skewness, and kurtosis across large return series is a natural array operation — NumPy vectorisation turns what would be slow iterative summations into single-call aggregations over the entire dataset.

label: implements
description: Simulating GBM paths requires generating correlated normal increments and computing cumulative products — vectorised NumPy expresses the entire path generation as array operations on random draws, avoiding per-step Python loops.

label: implements
description: The pandas ewm() method implements exponentially weighted moving average calculations, including EWMA volatility — the same lambda-weighted variance estimator used in RiskMetrics and other risk models.

label: implements analysis of
description: pandas provides the vectorised methods (pct_change, rolling, resample) used to compute and analyse the statistical properties of returns — mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis, and autocorrelation — directly from price series.

label: computes
description: pandas rolling().std() computes the standard deviation over a moving window — the same variance and standard deviation concepts applied incrementally across a time series to produce rolling volatility estimates.

label: requires
description: The covariance matrix passed to the portfolio optimiser must be positive semi-definite for the quadratic objective to be convex and the minimum-variance solution to exist — if the sample covariance is not PSD (due to missing data or numerical issues), the optimiser may fail or produce nonsensical weights.

label: implements
description: Portfolio optimisation in Python traces the efficient frontier computationally by repeatedly solving the constrained minimisation at a grid of target returns — the numerical counterpart to Markowitz's analytical frontier.

label: implements
description: SciPy's constrained optimiser is the practical tool for implementing Markowitz mean-variance optimisation — it finds the optimal asset weights that modern portfolio theory defines analytically but which require numerical methods for real-world portfolios with many assets and constraints.

label: aggregates
description: A random forest combines B independently trained decision trees by averaging (regression) or voting (classification) — the ensemble variance is 1/B times the single-tree variance when trees are uncorrelated.

label: example of
description: Random forests are one of the most widely used ML algorithms in finance due to their robustness to outliers, ability to handle non-linear interactions, and built-in feature importance scores for factor selection.

label: validated via OOB error analogous to
description: The out-of-bag error provides a free cross-validation estimate for random forests — each tree is evaluated on the ~37% of data excluded from its bootstrap sample, avoiding the look-ahead bias risk of standard k-fold CV on financial time series.

label: applies
description: Cholesky decomposition is used in random number generation to convert independent standard normal draws into correlated draws — multiplying by the lower-triangular Cholesky factor of the correlation matrix produces the required joint distribution.

label: generates inputs for
description: Random number generation produces the stochastic draws that drive Monte Carlo simulation — each simulated path begins with a vector of pseudorandom normal variates that are transformed into asset price movements.

label: implements numerically
description: Random number generation with Cholesky-correlated normal draws is the numerical implementation of correlated Brownian motions — each column of the transformed random matrix represents a discrete approximation to a correlated Wiener process.

label: calibrates
description: SciPy root-finding (brentq) is the standard numerical method for inverting the Black-Scholes formula to extract implied volatility from observed option prices — the most common calibration task in derivatives.

label: computes
description: Implied volatility cannot be solved analytically from the Black-Scholes formula — SciPy root-finding algorithms (brentq, newton) numerically invert the pricing equation to recover the market-implied vol from each observed option price.

label: fits
description: Calibrating a stochastic volatility model (Heston, SABR) to the observed volatility surface requires minimising the pricing error across many strikes and expiries simultaneously — SciPy minimize orchestrates this multi-parameter optimisation.

label: requires
description: Pricing an American option requires evaluating the early exercise decision at every node — the option price is the maximum of the intrinsic value and the continuation value.

label: extends
description: An American option is worth at least as much as its European counterpart — the early-exercise premium is the additional value from the right to exercise before expiry.

label: compares
description: The comparison card contrasts the pricing methodologies, exercise rights, and put-call parity relationships that distinguish American from European options.

label: compares
description: The comparison card highlights that European options admit closed-form Black-Scholes prices and exact put-call parity, serving as the analytically tractable baseline.

label: constrains
description: Put-call parity holds exactly for European options but only as inequality bounds for American options — the early-exercise right breaks the strict equality.

label: alternative to
description: The Bachelier model is the normal-distribution alternative to Black-Scholes' lognormal framework — it predates Black-Scholes by 73 years and has returned to prominence for pricing options on negative interest rates.

label: prices
description: Swaption markets now routinely quote Bachelier (normal) implied volatility alongside or instead of Black implied volatility, especially for EUR and JPY rates that can be negative.

label: applies
description: The Bachelier model embodies the normal side of the normal-vs-lognormal choice — it assumes arithmetic Brownian motion (normal prices) rather than geometric (lognormal), allowing the underlying to go negative.

label: extended by
description: American option pricing uses backward induction with an additional early-exercise check at each node — the algorithm compares the continuation value against the immediate exercise payoff and keeps the maximum.

label: computes
description: At each node of the backward pass, the hedge ratio (delta) is computed as the ratio of the change in option value to the change in stock price across the two successor nodes, giving the exact number of shares needed to replicate the option.

label: prices
description: Backward induction on a binomial tree prices European options by discounting terminal payoffs step-by-step to the present — as the number of steps grows, the price converges to the Black-Scholes closed form.

label: implements
description: Each backward step applies the one-period risk-neutral pricing formula — discounting the probability-weighted average of successor values at the risk-free rate — making backward induction a discrete implementation of risk-neutral valuation.

label: priced by
description: The cash-or-nothing binary call price Q·e^(-rT)·N(d₂) is derived directly from the Black-Scholes framework — N(d₂) is the risk-neutral probability that the option expires in the money.

label: models payoff of
description: A merger arbitrage position has a binary payoff structure — a fixed profit if the deal closes, a fixed loss if it breaks — making the binary option the natural instrument for pricing and hedging deal risk.

label: illustrates
description: The binary call price equals the discounted risk-neutral probability of finishing in the money, making it the clearest illustration of the risk-neutral pricing principle: price = e^(-rT)·E^Q[payoff].

label: converges to
description: As the number of time steps N tends to infinity, the binomial tree converges to the continuous-time Black-Scholes model — this is the Cox-Ross-Rubinstein convergence result.

label: derives
description: The hedge ratio (delta) emerges naturally within the binomial framework as the ratio of the spread in option values to the spread in stock prices at each node.

label: derives
description: The binomial model derives the hedge ratio as the ratio of the option payoff spread to the stock price spread at each node — this discrete formula converges to the Black-Scholes delta in the continuous-time limit.

label: demonstrates
description: The binomial model is the simplest demonstration of risk-neutral pricing — the real-world probability cancels out and only the risk-neutral probability p-prime enters the pricing formula.

label: defines
description: The Black-Scholes formula provides the closed-form expression for Delta as N(d1) for calls and N(d1)-1 for puts, making it the standard reference for computing and interpreting Delta.

label: enables
description: Black-Scholes shows that a continuously rebalanced Delta hedge eliminates all price risk, which is precisely the argument used to derive the Black-Scholes PDE.

label: prices
description: The Black-Scholes formula gives closed-form prices for European calls and puts — it is the standard analytical pricing tool for European-style options. The closed-form Black–Scholes formula applies only to European-style options because it assumes the terminal payoff is the sole boundary condition — American options introduce a free boundary (early exercise) that the PDE cannot handle without numerical methods.

label: defines
description: The Black-Scholes formula gives the closed-form expression for Gamma as N prime(d1) / (S sigma sqrt(T)), the second derivative of the option price with respect to the underlying.

label: defines
description: The Black-Scholes formula gives closed-form expressions for Delta, Gamma, Vega, Theta and Rho — the Greeks are simply partial derivatives of the Black-Scholes price with respect to each input.

label: defines
description: The Black-Scholes formula gives the closed-form expression for Rho as KTe^{-rT}N(d2) for calls, the sensitivity of the option price to changes in the risk-free rate.

label: defines
description: The Black-Scholes formula provides the closed-form expression for Theta, and the Black-Scholes PDE directly links Theta to Gamma via the relationship Theta + 0.5 Gamma sigma^2 S^2 = rV.

label: defines
description: The Black-Scholes formula gives the closed-form expression for Vega as S N prime(d1) sqrt(T), the sensitivity of the option price to changes in implied volatility.

label: inverted to get
description: Implied volatility is found by running Black-Scholes in reverse: given a market price for an option, you solve numerically for the volatility that makes the formula match.

label: enforces
description: Cash-and-carry arbitrage is the mechanism that enforces the no-arbitrage forward price — any deviation from F* = S₀e^(rT) is immediately exploited, driving the forward price back to fair value.

label: exploits mispricing of
description: Cash-and-carry arbitrage exploits a mispricing between the spot price and the forward price — the strategy buys at spot and sells forward, locking in a riskless profit whenever the basis exceeds the cost of carry.

label: applies
description: The fair forward price F* = S₀e^(rT) is derived directly from the time value of money — carrying the spot asset to expiry costs exactly the compounded risk-free interest on the purchase price.

label: analogous to
description: Charm and Colour are both time-decay Greeks for spot sensitivities: Charm measures how Delta drifts with time, while Colour measures how Gamma drifts with time.

label: refines
description: Charm tells a hedger how much their Delta will drift with the passage of time, enabling them to anticipate and pre-adjust hedge ratios over weekends and holidays when no rebalancing is possible.

label: assumed by
description: The Black-Scholes equation is derived under the assumption of a complete market — continuous trading in the stock and bond is sufficient to replicate any European payoff, yielding a unique price.

label: enables
description: Market completeness is the theoretical guarantee that delta hedging works: because every contingent claim can be replicated, the hedging portfolio perfectly offsets the option exposure.

label: guarantees uniqueness of
description: In a complete market the risk-neutral measure is unique — completeness plus no-arbitrage together ensure there is exactly one pricing measure, which is the second Fundamental Theorem of Asset Pricing.

label: derives
description: Taking the continuous-time limit of the binomial model produces the Black-Scholes PDE — this card shows how the discrete one-step formula converges to the continuous PDE as the time step shrinks to zero.

label: enforced by
description: Cash-and-carry arbitrage is the enforcement mechanism of the cost-of-carry relationship — when the forward price deviates from the theoretical carry price, arbitrageurs buy spot and sell the forward (or vice versa) to restore equilibrium.

label: generalises
description: The cost-of-carry model generalises the forward price formula by explicitly incorporating storage costs and convenience yield alongside financing costs, making it applicable to commodities and physical assets beyond simple financial forwards.

label: derived from
description: The cost-of-carry pricing formula is a direct application of the no-arbitrage principle — if the forward price differed from spot plus net carry costs, a riskless profit would be available, violating no-arbitrage.

label: includes
description: The funding rate is the financing component of cost-of-carry — the interest paid to borrow cash (or received on collateral) is the dominant carry cost for financial assets and directly enters the forward pricing formula.

label: arguments of
description: d₁ and d₂ are the two arguments fed into the normal CDF in the Black-Scholes formula — understanding what they encode (log-moneyness, drift, and volatility scaling) is essential to interpreting and differentiating the formula.

label: standardises
description: d₁ and d₂ standardise the option's moneyness by dividing log(S/K) by the volatility-scaled time horizon σ√T, converting moneyness into a z-score suitable for normal CDF evaluation.

label: arguments passed to
description: d₁ and d₂ are the specific values passed to N(·) and N'(·) in the Black-Scholes formula — this notation card explains how to read the CDF and PDF symbols that appear in the pricing and Greek expressions.

label: contains
description: The σ²/2 term that distinguishes d₁ from d₂ is the volatility drag from geometric Brownian motion — it reflects the gap between the arithmetic and geometric mean returns of lognormally distributed assets.

label: extended by
description: Speed provides the third-order correction term to the Delta-Gamma approximation, improving accuracy for large moves where the quadratic approximation breaks down.

label: used in
description: The Delta-Gamma approach enables faster VaR computation for options portfolios by approximating the P&L distribution with a quadratic function of the underlying rather than full repricing.

label: differentiated to give
description: Charm is the time derivative of Delta — it measures how the hedge ratio changes purely from the passage of time, even when the underlying price remains unchanged.

label: first term of
description: Delta provides the first-order (linear) term of the approximation — it captures the directional component of the option s P&L for small moves in the underlying.

label: foundation of
description: Delta is the hedge ratio itself — delta hedging works by holding exactly Delta units of the underlying to offset the option s first-order price sensitivity, so understanding Delta is a prerequisite for constructing any delta-neutral portfolio.

label: differentiated to give
description: Gamma is the derivative of Delta with respect to the underlying price — it measures how quickly the hedge ratio changes and therefore how often a delta hedge must be rebalanced.

label: cross-derivative of
description: Vanna measures how Delta changes with implied volatility — it is the cross partial derivative linking the directional sensitivity (Delta) to the volatility dimension.

label: requires management of
description: Delta hedging a long option position creates a short gamma position — the portfolio must be rebalanced as the underlying moves, making Gamma the primary measure of hedging frequency and cost.

label: underlies
description: The Black–Scholes model derives the option price by constructing a delta-hedged portfolio that earns the risk-free rate — the no-arbitrage principle ensures the price is unique and consistent.

label: exposed to
description: Delta hedging is only perfect in a continuous-time world with zero friction and constant volatility — discrete rebalancing, transaction costs, and volatility jumps are the primary limitations that break the hedge in practice.

label: informed by
description: Theta and the cost of carry determine whether early exercise is worthwhile — when Theta decay exceeds the interest earned on the intrinsic value, continuing to hold is optimal.

label: driven by
description: The decision to exercise an American option early depends on whether the benefit of immediate exercise exceeds the remaining time value — Theta quantifies the rate at which that time value is disappearing.

label: driven by
description: The core trade-off in early exercise is whether receiving cash now and earning interest outweighs the option's remaining time value — a direct application of the time value of money.

label: valued via
description: European option prices equal the discounted expected payoff under the risk-neutral measure — this expectation is the defining calculation for European-style derivatives.

label: extends
description: Expiration P&L extends the payoff diagram by subtracting the premium paid, shifting the payoff curve down by the initial cost and revealing the break-even price.

label: depends on
description: The premium paid or received is the cost basis in the expiration P&L calculation — decomposing it into intrinsic and time value explains why the break-even differs from the strike.

label: evaluates
description: Expiration P&L on a long call equals the call's terminal payoff minus the premium, providing the simplest measure of whether buying the call was a profitable trade.

label: accuracy described by
description: The truncation error of a finite difference scheme — forward, backward, or central — is described using big-O notation: the central difference has O(Δx²) error whilst the one-sided schemes have O(Δx) error, directly informing the choice of scheme in option pricing grids.

label: used for
description: American option pricing requires finding an early-exercise boundary as part of the solution — finite difference methods handle this naturally by working backwards through the grid and applying the early-exercise condition at each node.

label: solves
description: Finite difference methods discretise the Black-Scholes PDE on a price-time grid and solve it backwards from the terminal payoff — they are the standard numerical approach when no closed-form solution exists, such as for American options.

label: derived from
description: The forward price formula is derived directly from the no-arbitrage principle — if the forward deviates from the cost-of-carry value, a riskless profit can be locked in via cash-and-carry or reverse cash-and-carry arbitrage.

label: foundation of
description: The forward price equals the risk-neutral expected spot price, making it the simplest example of risk-neutral pricing — understanding forwards builds intuition for the Q-measure approach to more complex derivatives.

label: derived from
description: The forward price is derived from the spot price via the cost-of-carry formula — it equals the spot price compounded at the risk-free rate, so the spot is the fundamental anchor of forward pricing.

label: applies
description: The forward price formula is a direct application of time value of money — it compounds the spot price at the risk-free rate to reflect the opportunity cost of capital over the contract life.

label: characterises
description: The Second Fundamental Theorem characterises market completeness: a market is complete if and only if the equivalent martingale measure is unique, guaranteeing a single no-arbitrage price for every claim.

label: formalises
description: The First Fundamental Theorem formalises the no-arbitrage principle: a market is arbitrage-free if and only if an equivalent martingale measure exists.

label: justifies
description: The FTAP provides the rigorous justification for risk-neutral pricing — the existence of ℚ (first theorem) means every derivative can be priced as a discounted ℚ-expectation.

label: requires
description: Both fundamental theorems are stated in terms of equivalent probability measures — the first requires existence of ℚ, the second links uniqueness of ℚ to market completeness.

label: differentiated to give
description: Colour is the time derivative of Gamma — it measures how Gamma drifts as time passes, just as Charm measures Delta decay and Veta measures Vega decay.

label: second term of
description: Gamma provides the second-order (quadratic) correction that accounts for the curvature of the option price, improving accuracy for larger moves where the linear Delta estimate breaks down.

label: governs cost of
description: Gamma determines how rapidly Delta shifts with the underlying, and therefore how frequently and expensively a delta hedge must be rebalanced — high Gamma means high hedging cost.

label: differentiated to give
description: Speed is the derivative of Gamma with respect to the underlying price — it measures how quickly Gamma shifts as spot moves, determining the stability of Gamma hedges.

label: trades off against
description: The Gamma-Theta trade-off is fundamental: for a delta-hedged portfolio the Black-Scholes PDE forces Theta + 0.5 Gamma sigma^2 S^2 = rV, so long Gamma positions necessarily bleed Theta and vice versa.

label: differentiated to give
description: Zomma is the derivative of Gamma with respect to implied volatility — it measures how the curvature of the option price shifts when volatility changes.

label: extends
description: Gamma scalping extends delta hedging from a risk-neutralisation technique into a profit-generating strategy — by repeatedly rebalancing the delta hedge, the trader monetises gamma into realised P&L that accumulates with each large move in the underlying.

label: monetises
description: Gamma scalping monetises the gamma of a long option position — the gamma value determines the dollar profit from each rehedge, making it the key Greek that drives the strategy's profitability.

label: implements
description: Gamma scalping is the primary practical implementation of a long volatility position — buying options and delta-hedging isolates the volatility bet, profiting when realised volatility exceeds the implied volatility paid.

label: execution method of
description: Gamma scalping is the execution method for volatility arbitrage — the trader expresses a view that realised volatility will differ from implied by buying or selling options and delta-hedging to isolate the volatility component.

label: numerical form of
description: Greeks by finite difference are the numerical implementation of differentiation rules — central differences approximate the derivative definition, with accuracy controlled by the bump size.

label: computes
description: Finite difference bumping is the universal method for computing option Greeks — it works with any pricing model by perturbing inputs and measuring the resulting price change.

label: feeds into
description: Greeks computed by finite difference feed directly into P&L attribution — the bumped sensitivities are multiplied by actual market moves to explain the daily P&L.

label: orchestrates
description: Greeks portfolio management orchestrates delta hedging at the book level — rather than hedging each option individually, the desk computes the net portfolio delta and hedges the aggregate, reducing transaction costs.

label: monitors
description: Greeks portfolio management monitors hedging error across the book — by tracking the residual Greeks after hedging, the desk can estimate the expected P&L variance from discrete rebalancing and prioritise which exposures to hedge first.

label: aggregates
description: Greeks portfolio management aggregates individual option Greeks into portfolio-level sensitivities — the position-weighted sum of deltas, gammas, vegas, and thetas determines the book's response to market moves.

label: coordinates
description: Greeks portfolio management coordinates vega hedging across the book — net vega is decomposed by expiry bucket and strike region to identify where volatility exposure is concentrated and which instruments to use for hedging.

label: applied in
description: The hedge ratio tells the trader how many shares to hold per option — delta hedging is the continuous rebalancing strategy that keeps this ratio current as the underlying moves.

label: uses
description: The minimum-variance hedge ratio h* = rho * sigma_S / sigma_F depends directly on the correlation between spot and futures price changes — low correlation means the hedge is less effective and the optimal ratio deviates further from one.

label: residual risk of
description: Hedging error is the residual risk that remains when delta hedging is performed discretely rather than continuously — each rebalancing interval accumulates a small P&L error proportional to gamma and the squared price move.

label: driven by
description: Hedging error is driven by gamma — high-gamma positions (near-the-money, near-expiry options) produce the largest rebalancing errors because the delta changes rapidly between hedge adjustments.

label: determines profitability of
description: The profitability of gamma scalping depends on keeping hedging error low enough that the gamma profits from discrete rebalancing are not eroded by the noise from imperfect hedge timing.

label: imperfection of
description: Hedging error quantifies the imperfection of option replication in practice — theoretical continuous replication has zero error, but discrete rebalancing introduces stochastic P&L deviations that scale inversely with the square root of rebalancing frequency.

label: implies
description: Put-call parity is a direct consequence of the law of one price: a call plus discounted cash equals a put plus stock, so both portfolios must cost the same to prevent arbitrage.

label: underpins
description: Risk-neutral pricing derives derivative values by constructing a replicating portfolio — the law of one price guarantees the derivative's price equals the replicating portfolio's cost.

label: violated by
description: A violation of the law of one price immediately implies a riskless arbitrage — buying the cheap portfolio and selling the expensive one at identical future payoffs generates a guaranteed profit with zero net investment.

label: qualifies
description: The no-arbitrage principle assumes a frictionless market, but limits to arbitrage explain why real prices can deviate from their theoretical no-arbitrage values within bounds set by transaction costs and funding spreads.

label: constrains
description: Real-world frictions such as transaction costs, funding constraints, and short-selling restrictions limit the profitability of riskless arbitrage, allowing small mispricings to persist that pure theory would predict should vanish instantly.

label: quantifies gap between
description: The market price of risk is the numerical quantity that separates ℙ from ℚ — it measures exactly how much excess drift per unit of volatility the measure change removes.

label: normalises
description: The market price of risk normalises the risk premium by volatility — θ = (μ − r)/σ expresses the risk premium per unit of risk rather than in absolute return terms.

label: equivalent to
description: The market price of risk is numerically identical to the Sharpe ratio — both equal (μ − r)/σ, measuring excess return per unit of volatility.

label: parameterises
description: The market price of risk θ = (μ − r)/σ is the parameter that Girsanov’s theorem uses to define the change of measure — it determines how much drift is absorbed into the new Brownian motion.

label: validates
description: The martingale test is the standard diagnostic that verifies a pricing implementation correctly produces the martingale property — it checks that Eᵠ[e^{−rT}S_T] ≈ S_0 across simulated paths.

label: validates
description: The martingale test is the first quality check run on a Monte Carlo engine — before trusting any option price, the quant verifies that discounted simulated prices average to the initial price.

label: instance of
description: The martingale test is a z-test of whether the sample mean of discounted terminal prices equals S₀ — it applies the standard hypothesis testing framework with H₀: implementation is correct.

label: grounded in
description: The entire martingale pricing framework is grounded in the fundamental theorems — the first theorem ensures ℚ exists (no arbitrage), the second ensures it is unique (complete market, unique prices).

label: operationalises
description: The martingale framework operationalises risk-neutral pricing — derivative values are computed as discounted ℚ-expectations precisely because discounted prices are martingales under ℚ.

label: uses for hedging
description: The martingale representation theorem guarantees that every contingent claim can be replicated by a dynamic trading strategy — the integrand in the representation is the delta hedge used on trading desks.

label: generalises
description: Merton's jump-diffusion pricing formula generalises Black-Scholes — when the jump intensity is zero the infinite series collapses to the single n=0 term and recovers the standard Black-Scholes price exactly.

label: prices
description: Merton's formula prices European options under jump-diffusion dynamics by expressing the option value as a Poisson-weighted sum of Black-Scholes prices, each conditioned on a specific number of jumps occurring before expiry.

label: uses
description: The Poisson distribution provides the probability weights in Merton's series — each term is weighted by the probability of exactly n jumps in time T, making the Poisson PMF the structural backbone of the formula.

label: prices under
description: Merton's option pricing formula is the closed-form solution for European options when the underlying follows the Merton jump-diffusion SDE — the pricing formula is the direct consequence of the jump-diffusion dynamics.

label: justified by
description: Delta hedging constructs the riskless portfolio whose existence the no-arbitrage principle requires — without a perfect hedge, the argument that the portfolio must earn exactly r collapses.

label: implies
description: Put-call parity is a direct consequence of no-arbitrage: if the relationship between a call, a put, the stock, and a bond were violated, a trader could construct a risk-free profit from the mispricing.

label: foundation of
description: The no-arbitrage condition guarantees the existence of a risk-neutral measure under which all discounted asset prices are martingales — this is the First Fundamental Theorem of Asset Pricing.

label: forbids
description: The no-arbitrage principle states that riskless arbitrage opportunities cannot persist in an efficient market — the principle is the axiom, and riskless arbitrage is the specific violation it rules out.

label: applied in
description: Triangular arbitrage is a direct application of the no-arbitrage principle to the FX market — it enforces the law of one price by ensuring the cost of a currency is independent of the conversion path.

label: generalises
description: Numéraire change generalises beyond the standard ℙ vs ℚ dichotomy to a family of measures — each tradeable asset can serve as numéraire, yielding a different measure under which a different quantity becomes a martingale.

label: creates
description: The key property of a numéraire change is that asset prices divided by the new numéraire become martingales under the new measure — choosing the natural numéraire makes the pricing expectation tractable.

label: uses
description: Each numéraire change is effected by a Radon–Nikodým derivative equal to the ratio of the new numéraire to the old, normalised by their initial values.

label: derived via
description: The Greeks are partial derivatives of the option price; a Taylor expansion in those derivatives gives a local approximation of how the portfolio P&L changes with small moves in price, time, and volatility.

label: includes
description: Colour is the third-order Greek measuring Gamma decay — it completes the time-decay picture alongside Charm (Delta decay) and Veta (Vega decay).

label: includes
description: Delta is the most fundamental of the option Greeks — it is the first partial derivative of the option price with respect to the underlying, and the starting point from which higher-order Greeks like Gamma are defined.

label: parameterises
description: Delta is the hedge ratio — it tells you exactly how many units of the underlying to hold in order to offset the option's directional exposure. Gamma measures how fast Delta changes with the underlying price — a high-Gamma position requires more frequent rebalancing of the delta hedge, making Gamma the key Greek for monitoring hedging cost and exposure between rebalances.

label: includes
description: Gamma is the second-order Greek in the Greeks family — it captures the curvature of the option price function that the first-order Delta approximation misses.

label: quantifies
description: Delta is the continuous-time hedge ratio — the option Greeks framework provides the exact number of underlying units needed to neutralise first-order price risk, which is the definition of the hedge ratio.

label: includes
description: Rho is the interest-rate sensitivity Greek — it completes the set of five first-order Greeks (Delta, Gamma, Theta, Vega, Rho) that describe an option s risk profile.

label: includes
description: Speed is the third-order spot Greek — it extends the Delta-Gamma-Speed hierarchy that describes the option price s Taylor expansion in the underlying to third order.

label: includes
description: Theta is the time-decay Greek — it measures how much option value erodes each day purely from the passage of time, and is a core component of any Greeks risk report.

label: includes
description: Vanna is the cross Greek linking Delta and Vega — it captures the interaction between directional and volatility risk that first-order Greeks miss.

label: includes
description: Vega is the volatility-sensitivity Greek — it measures how much an option s price changes per unit change in implied volatility, making it essential for volatility trading strategies.

label: includes
description: Veta is the Vega-time cross Greek — it measures the rate of Vega decay and is essential for managing calendar spreads where differential Vega decay drives profitability.

label: includes
description: Volga is the second-order volatility Greek — it measures the convexity of the option price with respect to implied volatility, extending the first-order sensitivity captured by Vega.

label: includes
description: Zomma is the cross Greek linking Gamma to volatility — it captures how the option s curvature shifts during volatility regime changes.

label: feeds into
description: In the Delta-Gamma VaR method, the option Greeks are used to approximate the portfolio loss as a polynomial of risk factor changes, allowing for rapid risk calculation without full revaluation.

label: determines behaviour of
description: Moneyness is the primary driver of an option's Greek profile — deep ITM options have delta near ±1 and minimal gamma, while ATM options have the highest gamma, theta, and vega exposure.

label: determines split of
description: Moneyness directly determines how the option premium splits between intrinsic and extrinsic value — ITM options carry both, while OTM options are entirely extrinsic.

label: classifies
description: Moneyness classifies a call as ITM, ATM, or OTM depending on whether the underlying exceeds, equals, or falls below the strike — this classification determines the call's intrinsic value and delta.

label: classifies
description: Moneyness classifies a put as ITM, ATM, or OTM depending on whether the underlying is below, at, or above the strike — this classification determines the put's intrinsic value and delta.

label: visualises
description: The protective put payoff diagram is constructed by vertically summing the long stock and long put payoff lines, revealing the characteristic kinked profile with a floor at the strike minus the premium.

label: visualises
description: The call payoff diagram plots the hockey-stick function max(S_T - K, 0), making the asymmetric risk-reward of a long call immediately visible.

label: visualises
description: The put payoff diagram plots the hockey-stick function max(K - S_T, 0), showing that the put holder profits as the underlying falls below the strike.

label: explains erosion of
description: Time decay is the mechanism by which the extrinsic (time value) component of the premium decomposition erodes to zero at expiry — understanding the split is prerequisite to understanding theta.

label: decomposes
description: The call premium decomposes into intrinsic value max(S - K, 0) plus extrinsic value — this split explains why an ITM call is worth more than its exercise value before expiry.

label: decomposes
